question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of Emergency Departments attendances during night-time economy hours, in 2025 in the (i) Belfast Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust, triaged as category (a) 4; and (b) 5, broken down by whether (i) no medical intervention was required; and (ii) the attendance could have been treated in an alternative setting by first aid.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of alcohol-related Emergency Departments attendances during night-time economy hours, in 2025 in the (i) Belfast Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust, triaged as category (a) 4; and (b) 5, broken down by whether (i) no medical intervention was required; and (ii) the attendance could have been treated in an alternative setting by first aid.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the cost of alcohol-related Emergency Departments attendances during night-time economy hours, in 2025 in the (i) Belfast Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust, triaged as category (a) 4; and (b) 5, broken down by whether (i) no medical intervention was required; and (ii) the attendance could have been treated in an alternative setting by first aid.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of ambulances that were called to Belfast city centre in 2025 for (i) alcohol-related incidents; and (ii) incidents requiring first aid, were no one was taken onwards to hospital.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the cost of an ambulance call out into Belfast city centre during the hours of the night-time economy.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service has provided an estimate of the average cost of an ambulance call out to Belfast city centre during hours associated with the night-time economy of £1,435.This estimate is based on the cost of providing an ambulance crew over a 12-hour shift and reflects direct operational costs only.  It does not include costs relating to dispatch and communications, capital expenditure or the apportionment of corporate management or other service overheads.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any additional staffing that would be required to operate a 24/7 thrombectomy service.
answer_text: My officials are working with the Belfast Trust to develop a business case to identify the resources required to support a 24/7 thrombectomy service. The business case will identify a preferred staffing model which will encompass a range of professions including Stroke Consultants, Nurses, Interventional and Diagnostic Radiology Consultants and Anaesthetic Consultants. It is anticipated that the business case will be finalised by the end of May 2026.While budgetary pressures have prevented me from allocating the additional funding needed to support a 24/7 service, I am confident on progress on a move to a 24/7 model being made soon.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for a breakdown of the £1.839 million annual funding allocated to the Regional Insulin Pump Service, including the amount directed to (i) the regional multidisciplinary team; (b) Belfast Health and Social Care Trust as the lead provider; and (iii) each of the other Health and Social Care Trusts for local delivery and support.
answer_text: The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence TA943 Hybrid closed loop systems for managing blood glucose levels in type 1 diabetes has been endorsed as applicable to Northern Ireland. Funding has been secured to support the implementation of a 5-year implementation plan through a service development of the Regional Insulin Pump service.  This will support equitable, sustainable and affordable access to this technology, which is targeted to optimise outcomes for those groups most likely to benefit. Once the commissioning process has been completed, further information, including a breakdown of the funding, will be available. However, the most appropriate system for each individual is the responsibility of the local Trust and their clinicians to determine in consultation with the patient and/or the patient’s carer.

To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has modelled the projected need of social care provision for the next 20 years.
answer_text: Northern Ireland’s social care system is experiencing increasing pressure due to demographic change, workforce shortages, and rising complexity of need. The proportion of the population aged 65+ is projected to continue rising significantly over the next two decades, with a particularly sharp increase expected among those aged 85+, who are the highest users of social care. My Department’s previous and current strategic reports have highlighted the need for longterm planning and modelling to ensure future sustainability.My Department, through the Social Care Collaborative Forum established in 2023 has already implemented Early Review Teams and Care Line Live to create efficiencies in homecare provision. Further work has commenced under the Sustainable Systems Implementation Group to consider a new model of homecare that is fit for the future.My Department established a Social Care Fair Work Forum in 2023 and I have given a commitment to making social care a real living wage sector.My Department completed a consultation on Adult Social Care Reform in 2024 which gathered public, provider, and sector feedback and has supported the case for a system-wide overhaul of social care services.The Social Care Workforce Strategy 2025–2035 sets out a vision and actions to develop a sustainable social care workforce over the next ten years.My Department has also been planning for the increase in demand for social care by considering investment in preventative care and early intervention, expansion of communitybased and agefriendly services, robust workforce planning and retention strategies, support for carers and digital innovation. The Department’s HSC Reset Plan, published on 9 July 2025, sets the immediate direction for stabilising and reforming the Health and Social Care (HSC) system. It outlines the initial threeyear strategy of Stabilisation, Reform and Delivery, with significant implications for adult social care.The Reset Plan commits to building a neighbourhoodcentred model of social care, embedding early intervention through integrated Neighbourhood Teams delivering services closer to home. The Plan emphasises enhanced use of digital tools and data, supporting better management of home care packages, improved scheduling, and more efficient deployment of staff. This aligns with digitalisation already underway across Trusts. The Plan outlines the need for a wholesystem, crossgovernment approach connecting health, housing, education and employment.Adult social care reform is ongoing, phased, and constrained by budget pressures, but the work I have referenced; SCCF, Fair Work Forum, Reset Plan, are now in place to progress delivery. Full system-wide reform will take time but early progress is happening.Social care already represents a substantial portion of Health and Social Care (HSC) expenditure. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the reason why the Overarching Winter Preparedness Plan 2025-26 has not resulted in meeting the two hour target on ambulance handovers.
answer_text: I set out in the published Winter Preparedness Plan, a target to improve ambulance handover and turnaround times and that this requires a collaborative approach between the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service and HSC Trusts. I welcome their ongoing partnership working and the development of a regional Ambulance Handover Protocol and reduced ambulance conveyances through increased use of alternative pathways.The expectation was that waits exceeding two hours should be eradicated by December 2025, and I can confirm that the regional average handover time for January 2026 was 1 hour 58 minutes. However, this improvement needs to be sustained and consistent. I have been clear that while winter preparedness can mitigate winter pressures, it cannot prevent them and the interplay of demand, capacity and flow are symptoms of whole system dynamics.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the impact that the £2.1 million reduction in expenditure in health service dentistry, cited as a part of savings and cost containment measures will have for NHS dentistry services.
answer_text: The number of registered dental patients in Northern Ireland continues to reduce and as reported in the Quarterly Family Practitioner Services (FPS) General Dental Statistics for Northern Ireland QE December 2025, published on 29 January 2026, registrations have decreased by a further 17.1% in the last year.This reduction has led to a reduction in the costs of registration fees paid to Dentists and in turn a reduction in the cost of treatments paid during the 2025/26 financial year and the realisation of savings of £2.1m.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action his Department is taking to strengthen medicine supply to Northern Ireland and prevent shortages, ahead of the expected reduced availability of certain medications.
answer_text: There are over 14,000 medicines licensed in the UK, and the majority are in good supply and are available in sufficient quantities to meet patient needs. However, medicines are manufactured as part of a highly regulated, complex and global industry; therefore, medicine supply disruptions are not uncommon and can occur for a variety of reasons such as manufacturing issues or increases in demand. The Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) leads on the maintenance of medicine supply chains to the UK and works closely with the devolved administrations, including my Department, the UK medicines regulator and other operators within the supply chain. Through national and local medicine shortage management groups, my Department is updated on any anticipated upcoming supply disruptions.If there is a medicine supply disruption, there are well established tools and processes that can be applied both locally and nationally to ensure patients can have access to the treatments they need. This includes providing healthcare professionals with advice and guidance on the actions they can take to ensure patients can access appropriate treatments. These communications include National Patient Safety Alerts, Medicine Supply Notifications and recommending healthcare professionals registering with the NHS Specialist Pharmacy Service (SPS) medicines supply tool. The SPS medicines supply tool provides up-to-date information on anticipated resupply dates, appropriate alternatives, clinical information and recommended actions to take based on the above advice.Medicines can also be placed on a list of medicines that cannot be exported from the UK or hoarded. This ensures all medicines within the UK can be made available to patients. Other tools and processes that can be utilised include the importation of medicines from other markets and Serious Shortage Protocols. All these actions have been considered and applied where appropriate to any anticipated upcoming supply disruption.While there are well established tools and processes that can be applied, I recognise the impact these supply disruptions can have on patients and on Health and Social Care providers in Northern Ireland. Consequently, I had previously written to Wes Streeting MP, Secretary of State for Health and Social Care, and Karin Smyth MP, Minister of Health (Secondary Care), highlighting my support for wider collaboration to improve long-term supply resilience across the UK.DHSC published a document in August 2025, entitled ‘Managing a robust and resilient supply of medicines’ which aims to reduce the frequency of shortages and minimise the impact on patients when they do occur. The document provides examples of collaboration between my Department, the UK Government and others to resolve some recent complex medicine supply disruptions. I welcome the aims of this publication and my Department is committed to working with our partners across the UK to find strategies to improve the security and supply of me

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether any assessment has been undertaken to determine if current school-based systems are adequate to deliver improved educational outcomes for neurodivergent pupils.
answer_text: My department fully appreciates the growing numbers of neurodiverse and neurodivergent children and young people across our schools and this needs to be reflected in our approach to all aspects of the education system from building design to curriculum, to teacher training, to assessment and to other areas of pupil supports.The Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Agenda and associated Delivery Plan, published February 2025, is the culmination of a comprehensive End to End Review of SEN provision from existing policy through to operational delivery via the Education Authority (EA) and other educational partners.   The Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan set out a range of actions proposed to transform the educational experience of all children with SEN including neurodivergent children and young people.  The EA early actions taken forward include: Publication and implementation of a child-centred Graduated Response Framework, and Implementation of child-centred Local IMPACT Teams.  The Graduated Response Framework provides guidance to schools and educational settings on how to identify and support children and young people with SEN/Disabilities.  The framework builds on established good practice already in schools and provides a flexible, child-centred approach, allowing movement between stages of the SEN Code of Practice based on need and progress. The EA’s Pupil Support Services transitioned to a regionally based Local IMPACT Teams model. This new network of localised multi-specialism teams offers a wide range of support to schools at both the whole-school level and Stage 1 and 2 of the SEN Code of Practice. This process is needs-led with provision is based on the presenting needs of the child or young person.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer (i) AQW 35303/22-27; (ii) AQW 35302/22-27; and (iii) AQW 32715/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the 17 December 2025 article released by the Royal College of Emergency Medicine that there were 1,122 deaths associated with long Emergency Department waits in 2024. (i) to detail any steps that are being taken to monitor and reduce mortality linked to these prolonged waits; and (ii) whether he will provide the number of deaths associated with long Emergency Department waits for 2025.
answer_text: I refer the Member to my previous response to AQW 33053/22-27 and AQW 35304/2227.I recognise the serious concerns raised in the Royal College of Emergency Medicine (RCEM) article of 17 December 2025 regarding mortality associated with long emergency department (ED) waits. Reducing delays for patients remains a key priority for my Department, and we continue to work closely with Health and Social Care Trusts, the RCEM and other professional bodies to improve patient flow and enhance safety across the unscheduled care pathway.With regard to the steps being taken to monitor and reduce mortality linked to prolonged ED waits, my Department is engaged with the Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) Emergency Medicine team and is aware of the Standard Mortality Ratio methodology used by RCEM to estimate excess deaths associated with ED stays of 12 hours or longer prior to admission. As outlined previously, establishing direct causality between prolonged waits and individual patient outcomes is highly complex and would require more detailed, patient-level information than is currently available.Nonetheless, my Department continues to monitor a range of indicators related to ED performance, patient flow and safety including crowding, ambulance handover times, time to initial assessment, and delays in admission. These measures enable Trusts to identify and respond to risks associated with extended waits.A wide programme of work is underway to reduce delays in EDs and thereby mitigate associated risks. This includes:expanding urgent care centres and minor injury units to provide safe and timely alternatives to ED attendance;the continued roll-out of Phone First services to support patients in accessing the most appropriate pathway;targeted actions to improve ambulance handover processes outside EDs; and,strengthening senior decision-making capacity, including the appointment of an additional 21 emergency medicine consultants over the past year, developed in collaboration with RCEM and Trusts.These actions form part of a system-wide approach to improve flow throughout hospitals, reduce congestion in EDs and support the timely delivery of safe and effective care.With regard to whether my Department will provide the number of deaths associated with long ED waits for 2025, as stated previously, my Department does not hold specific data on the number of deaths associated with long waits in EDs, nor does it routinely collect the detailed information required to calculate excess mortality using the Standard Mortality Ratio approach. While my Department continues to consider the methodologies used elsewhere, such analyses require a level of granularity in patient tracking and outcome attribution that is not currently available within our systems.My Department will continue to work with Trusts, GIRFT and professional bodies to strengthen data quality and to identify opportunities to improve monitoring and analysis as part of ongoing service improvement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of the decision by NHS England to expand routine access to abiraterone for prostate cancer, and with similar availability in Scotland and Wales, to detail when this treatment will be available in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department is aware that NHS England (NHSE) have adopted a commissioning position on the use of abiraterone (Zytiga®) for the treatment of high-risk, hormone-sensitive, non-metastatic prostate cancer in England based on the availability of a generic version of the medicine, however, my Department does not currently have a formal policy position on access to medicines recommended through an NHSE commissioning policy, such as is the case with the use of abiraterone (Zytiga®) for the treatment of high-risk, hormone-sensitive, non-metastatic prostate cancer in England.  My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. This link has ensured that Northern Ireland has access to up-to-date, independent, professional, evidence-based guidance on the value of health care interventions. In practice this means that treatments that have been recommended by NICE for routine use in the NHS in England are also routinely available in Northern Ireland.Where NICE has recommended the use of abiraterone (Zytiga®) in the treatment of prostate cancer, this medicine is available to eligible patients in Northern Ireland, in line with the current NICE guidelines. Further information on this treatment is available at the following links:https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ta259https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ta387https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ta951https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ta1110In circumstances where NICE is yet to complete a full appraisal, or does not recommend a particular drug, the Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) does not routinely commission that therapy for use in Northern Ireland. Northern Ireland does not have its own health technology assessment body or equivalent to NICE, Scottish Medicines Consortium or All Wales Medicine Strategy Group, that could make a bespoke determination for Northern Ireland patients in the absence of NICE guidance.My Department has recently published a review of HSC medicines access arrangements which recognises the need to take action to ensure continued access to clinically and cost-effective medicines for the Northern Ireland population on an equitable basis, including in cases where NICE have not made a determination, but use is supported by clinical evidence and best practice. Informed by the review’s recommendations, my Department plans to review the existing workforce infrastructure underpinning medicines access pathways to identify potential resource requirements needed to strengthen existing access arrangements, including for use of off-label and off-patent medicines where use is supported by clinical evidence and best practice. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) how health economic analysis is incorporated into his Department's decision making, including service prioritisation and budget decisions; (ii) the number of staff within his Department who hold professional accreditation, specifically in health economics; and (iii) his assessment of whether the level of expertise is comparable to arrangements in other UK health departments, in terms of specialist qualifications in health economics.
answer_text: In light of the constrained financial environment that the Department of Health (DoH) has been operating under for several years, most of the additional funding that becomes available is directed towards addressing existing inescapable pressures rather than funding new initiatives. All decisions with funding implications are subject to scrutiny through the normal business case process, with proposals above delegated approval limits reviewed by Departmental Economists alongside other policy and analytical specialists.Economists working within the Department of Health are part of the wider Northern Ireland Civil Service Economist Group. They must possess at least a second-class honours degree with Economics as a major component. They bring broad skills and experience in public sector economics and contribute to budget and spending decisions through the business case process. A health economics qualification is a postgraduate or professional certification. No need has been identified within the Department for Economists to be employed with such specific qualifications.Given the scale of the financial pressures facing the Health and Social Care system, it would not represent an effective use of the limited resources available to undertake a review of how DoH compares with the arrangements in other UK health departments in terms of specific health economics qualifications.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the longest ambulance wait outside a Emergency Department in December 2025.

Through the Overarching Winter Preparedness Plan 2025–26, a comprehensive set of actions has been developed to address these challenges.With regard to improving ambulance handover times, in August 2025 a regional workshop involving Trusts and the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) was convened, drawing on best practice from the London Ambulance Service and King George Hospital. Based on this, new ambulance handover guidance is being finalised. Phase 1 thresholds, agreed with Trusts, aimed to eliminate ambulance handover delays exceeding two hours by 1 December 2025. Trusts are also working to prevent deterioration in shorter delay thresholds (15, 30, and 60 minutes), with a longer-term objective of achieving the 15-minute standard.In addition to the focus on eliminating delays exceeding two hours, Trusts have been asked to reduce delays in excess of 12 hours in EDs by a minimum of 10% when compared to the same period last year. Belfast Trust has made significant progress in this regard, having achieved a 12.5% reduction in the first 9 months of the year. Whilst progress has not been as significant in other Trusts, Northern Trust achieved a 7.5% reduction in Quarter 3, Western Trust achieved a 5% reduction and South Eastern Trust achieved a 2.5% reduction. Whilst the Southern Trust did not see a reduction, they have seen a significant improvement in Quarter 3 compared to the first six months. This is the first year where the trend has been reversed.Trusts and NIAS are implementing a range of measures aimed at reducing these extended delays, including additional short stay and intermediate care beds, enhanced front door frailty and multidisciplinary assessments, the expansion of Same Day Emergency Care pathways, and strengthened discharge co-ordination. These actions are intended not only to improve performance against the two hour threshold but also to minimise the occurrence of the longest delays.With regard to NIAS’ capacity and system flow, to support improved ambulance offload and response times, my Department has repurposed £10 million to fund targeted initiatives across Trusts. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the average ambulance turnaround time at Emergency Departments in December 2025; and (ii) the performance of the Emergency Departments against the two-hour ambulance turnaround target in the Overarching Winter Preparedness Plan 2025-26.
answer_text: Table 1 shows both the average and the longest ambulance handover time for December 2025 at each emergency department (ED) and the percentage of ambulance arrivals with a handover delay over the 2-hour maximum threshold set out in the 2025/26 Overarching Winter Preparedness Plan.Table 1Dec-25	Average ambulance handover time(hh:mm:ss)	Longest ambulance handover time(hh:mm:ss)	% arrivals with delays over 2 hoursAltnagelvin	01:06:51	07:54:52	12%Antrim Area	01:44:06	15:26:51	29%Causeway	01:39:19	16:31:53	26%Craigavon	01:46:47	15:40:44	25%Daisyhill	01:06:00	05:21:45	15%Mater	00:54:41	10:39:13	8%RBSC	00:25:01	01:36:37	0%RVH	01:11:18	10:08:44	17%South West	01:01:16	07:12:08	9%Ulster	02:28:02	19:04:06	40%My Department recognises the significant pressures faced by EDs and the impact these have on ambulance handover times and overall system flow. Through the Overarching Winter Preparedness Plan 2025–26, a comprehensive set of actions has been developed to address these challenges.With regard to improving ambulance handover times, in August 2025 a regional workshop involving Trusts and the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) was convened, drawing on best practice from the London Ambulance Service and King George Hospital. Based on this, new ambulance handover guidance is being finalised. Phase 1 thresholds, agreed with Trusts, aimed to eliminate ambulance handover delays exceeding two hours by 1 December 2025. Trusts are also working to prevent deterioration in shorter delay thresholds (15, 30, and 60 minutes), with a longer-term objective of achieving the 15-minute standard.In addition to the focus on eliminating delays exceeding two hours, Trusts have been asked to reduce delays in excess of 12 hours in EDs by a minimum of 10% when compared to the same period last year. Belfast Trust has made significant progress in this regard, having achieved a 12.5% reduction in the first 9 months of the year. Whilst progress has not been as significant in other Trusts, Northern Trust achieved a 7.5% reduction in Quarter 3, Western Trust achieved a 5% reduction and South Eastern Trust achieved a 2.5% reduction. Whilst the Southern Trust did not see a reduction, they have seen a significant improvement in Quarter 3 compared to the first six months. This is the first year where the trend has been reversed.Trusts and NIAS are implementing a range of measures aimed at reducing these extended delays, including additional short stay and intermediate care beds, enhanced front door frailty and multidisciplinary assessments, the expansion of Same Day Emergency Care pathways, and strengthened discharge co-ordination. These actions are intended not only to improve performance against the two hour threshold but also to minimise the occurrence of the longest delays.With regard to NIAS’ capacity and system flow, to support improved ambulance offload and response times, my Department has repurposed £10 million to fund targeted initiatives across Trusts. To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount of the medical negligence settlement expenditure paid by Health and Social Care Trusts (i) in 2024-25, totalling over £69 million; and (ii) in 2023-24, totalling over £41 million, related to cases where liability was accepted.
answer_text: Medical Negligence claims are managed by the Department of Legal Services (DLS) within the Business Services Organisation on behalf of HSC organisations. Expenditure figures for medical negligence settlements include payments both in cases where liability was formally accepted and in cases where no admission of liability was made. However, as analysis of whether liability is formally accepted or where no admission of liability was made is not captured in the current DLS database, it is not possible to provide details of the amount of medical negligence settlement expenditure paid by Health and Social Care Trusts related to cases where liability was accepted in 2023-24 and 2024-25.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail who will decide and set the time bound, measurable actions targeted to progress the Disability Strategy outcomes mentioned in his statement to the Assembly today.
answer_text: The draft Disability Strategy includes a range of measures which will be considered and reported on throughout its lifespan to give a holistic picture of where the Strategy is working and, just as importantly, where it’s not. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of GP practices that are receiving support from the Practice Improvement and Crisis Response Team.
answer_text: The aim of the Practice Improvement and Crisis Response Team is to support GP Practices at risk, to develop and implement an effective Practice Recovery Plan to ensure delivery of GMS services for their registered patients. This will include advice on management issues and GP clinical cover.The service is provided through GP Federations and was established to support GP Practices at risk and to develop and implement an effective Practice Recovery Plan.  The Federation-led General Practice Improvement and Crisis Response Team incorporates expert General Practice managerial support at short notice for practices at high risk or in a crisis and improved access to clinical GP cover at short notice for practices at high risk or in a crisis. As at 18 December 2025, 29 GP Practices are recorded as currently receiving support from the Practice Improvement and Crisis Response Team across Northern Ireland. Of these 24 practices are receiving GP clinical support, 3 are receiving managerial support and 2 practice improvement support.Previous figures show that in February 2024 a total of 40 practices were supported, with the numbers rising to 43 in June 2024.December 2024 saw 29 practices receive support, with 30 in February and June 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans his Department’s has to conduct a primary evidence review of gambling-related harms.
answer_text: It is well documented that Northern Ireland currently has a deficit in up-to-date data and evidence on gambling harms. To address this, my Department has worked collaboratively with cross-departmental colleagues to strengthen the evidence base. This includes the inclusion of gambling-related questions in the Young Person’s Behaviour and Attitude Survey 2022 (YPBAS) and, for the first time, in a pilot module, in the Northern Ireland Health Survey 2023/24.  The results from the YPBAS were published on 6 December 2023 and the Northern Ireland Health Survey pilot results were published on 11 December 2025.   In addition, the Department for Communities commissioned a Gambling Prevalence Survey during 2024, with results published earlier this year. Whilst there are no plans to conduct a formal primary evidence review, my officials will continue to work with officials in the Department for Communities to identify the most appropriate source of gambling prevalence and harm information for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will introduce screening programmes across all health and social care services to identify and assist people with gambling problems.
answer_text: In line with the usual process for commissioning services, any decision to commission bespoke gambling treatment services in the future would need to be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, to create a fuller understanding of the population need in Northern Ireland and in the context of future budget availability.  Funding for services aimed at prevention and treatment of gambling harm would require new or additional funding.In order to develop a fuller understanding of the population need in Northern Ireland, my Department is working collaboratively with officials in the Department for Communities to build an evidence base on the level of problem gambling in Northern Ireland and its associated harms.  Whilst there are currently no plans to introduce screening programmes in Northern Ireland to identify and assist people with gambling problems, my officials will consider the options for the implementation of recommendations in the All Party Group report on gambling, including screening, should funding become available.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how their Department will ensure the Equality Commission for Northern Ireland is adequately funded to carry out its statutory duties, including for any obligations under evolving equality standards.

To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any actions his Department is taking to expand the availability of accessible and appropriate housing for people with disabilities, in light of the shortfall in suitable provision.
answer_text: The Executive Housing Supply Strategy, developed following extensive consultation with stakeholders including disability organisations, highlights the need for more affordable and accessible homes that meet a diverse range of needs.My Department is working with Executive colleagues and the Housing Executive to develop the first Strategy Action Plan focused on practical, deliverable solutions to such issues. Alongside this, in the delivery of the Social Housing Development Programme, all new build social homes are currently built to ‘Lifetime Homes Standards’, ensuring that homes can be easily adapted to meet the changing needs of tenants. Housing Associations are also asked to include wheelchair accessible units within all development proposals.My Department also provides funding, administered by the Housing Executive, for Disabled Facilities Grants to adapt the home of a disabled person who is living in the private sector.  We are working  with the Department of Health and the Housing Executive on  a review of these grants to identify ways to improve this service. We also fund the Supporting People Programme, which provides housing-related support that helps people, including those with disabilities, to live on their own. This year, I allocated an additional £1 million for pipeline schemes in 2025/26 to fund new and extended Supporting People services. I have just given my approval for the Housing Executive to launch a public consultation on proposed amendments to the House Sales Scheme.  This will include exclusion of adapted or accessible dwellings which have undergone major adaptations from the Scheme so this accommodation remains available for people with disabilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on his actions to establish an Independent Environmental Protection Agency.
answer_text: In 2020, New Decade New Approach had a commitment to set up an independent Environmental Protection Agency. There have also been two Assembly motions endorsing it in 2020 and in 2025. The independent expert panel have most recently provided unequivocal support for an independent EPA, in their report published in October 2025. However, despite this context and the clear and significant damage that failed environmental governance is having on our environment, our economy, people’s livelihoods, and crucially public trust and confidence, I am still seeking Executive support, in principle, to establish an independent Environmental Protection Agency in Northern Ireland.  I am not alone in finding this hugely frustrating when we consider the costs of inaction and the overwhelming support for change which will benefit everyone. I would urge my Executive colleagues to provide the support needed.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on her plans to allow broadcasting in courts.
answer_text: ANSWER – AQO 2799/22-27I believe that the broadcasting of certain court proceedings, such as legal exchanges and judgments in the Court of Appeal, and sentencing remarks in the Crown Court, will be a positive step in enhancing the transparency of the justice system.  I wish to emphasise again that I am not proposing to allow the recording or filming of victims, witnesses, defendants or jurors, nor indeed members of the public or court staff.  Broadcasting will, in essence, simply provide another way for journalists to cover proceedings on which they can already report, and any reporting restrictions would still apply.  A report, summarising the response to the broadcasting of courts consultation, was published at the end of October.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the timeline for agreeing a multi-year budget for the Executive.
answer_text: As previously outlined in the Chamber, I will bring proposals on a draft Budget to the Executive in December, following the Chancellors Autumn Budget.   Subject to the Executive’s agreement, this will allow for a 12-week public consultation to launch in December, shortly after the draft Budget is agreed.Responses to this public consultation will help shape the Executive considerations in agreeing a final multi-year Budget which will set Resource allocations for the next three years and Capital allocations for the next four years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given the Joint Committee on Vaccination and Immunisation (JCVI) keeps its advice on seasonal flu vaccination programmes under regular review, to detail (i) what contingency plans are in place should the JCVI change its guidelines; and (ii) to detail within what timeframe could his Department respond to any change.
answer_text: The JCVI aims to provide a clear framework for the annual influenza (flu) vaccination programme in advance, meeting every June and October to provide its advice on the following year's flu vaccination programme.  This allows sufficient time for policy considerations, approvals, and procurement processes to be completed for the following year.The JCVI does, however, retain the flexibility to adapt its advice based on the dynamic nature of flu viruses and emerging scientific evidence. On rare occasions, the JCVI may change its flu advice at short notice in response to emerging evidence, significant changes in the epidemiological situation (e.g., emergence of a new, highly transmissible or virulent strain), or issues with vaccine supply or availability.The timescales within which my Department can respond to changing JCVI advice will vary depending on when the advice is published and the specifics of the advice. For example, during the 2023/24 programme the influenza and COVID-19 campaigns were implemented over a shorter condensed period in response to the emergence of a new COVID-19 variant.The influenza strains in vaccine changes from year to year and therefore sufficient quantities of flu vaccine are purchased based on the JCVI advice issued in October the previous year.  This is to avoid excess vaccine being wasted at the end of each flu season.  Should the JCVI recommend an expansion of the current flu vaccination programme at this late stage, the PHA, which manages the supply of vaccines for the programme, would balance availability with demand via ordering controls, allowing for rapid adjustments to distribution. However if, the purchase of additional influenza vaccine stock was necessary, the PHA, (along with other UK administrations), would need to seek this from manufacturers at short notice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans in place to increase vaccination uptake rates across the population.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency (PHA) is responsible for the operational delivery of all national vaccination programmes in Northern Ireland, and it employs a range of activities to support and promote vaccination uptake.  The PHA has established an Improving Childhood Vaccine Uptake Working Group to identify barriers to uptake and implement actions to improve uptake in childhood vaccination programmes to prevent vaccine-preventable diseases and potential outbreaks in Northern Ireland.  Actions taken include: Additional training for vaccinators in the use of the Child Health System to call eligible children appropriately;Implementation of core immunisation training for vaccinators through the Clinical Education Centre (including training on communication and motivational interviewing);The creation of additional public resources for cohorts where uptake is known to be low, including the provision of materials in different languages and for populations where literacy may be lower;Liaison with the Education Authority to promote awareness of the importance vaccination within educational settings;Providing training and resources for Sure Start leads to promote vaccinations in pre-school settings;Providing GPs with advisory letters highlighting their practice uptake and comparisons to other GPs in their area; andCollaborating with charities such as Cancer Focus and Meningitis Research Foundation to promote associated vaccination programmes. In addition, the PHA conducts school visits, promotes immunisation programmes in undergraduate and postgraduate medical and nursing settings, and hosts webinars for professional groups including GPs, community pharmacies, teachers and the NI Childminder’s association.With regard to seasonal vaccination programmes, the PHA has deployed the following:A review of vaccine uptake by school and Super Output Level to facilitate targeted action to low uptake areas to address convenience and confidence in vaccination programmes; Provision of professional training slides to support vaccinating staff and of hard copy and online patient information material as part of informed consent; Provision of social media resources (including infographics, “flu virus watch” statistics and school’s information);Liaison with the Education Authority to promote flu vaccination through schools; and The publication of vaccination uptake rates on the PHA website to share performance.In addition, and because of indications of an earlier than usual influenza season, the PHA will shortly be rolling out a paid-for communications campaign to promote the flu vaccination programme and encourage uptake by all eligible groups. The ‘Stay Well This Winter’’ campaign supported by social media and printed materials, is also currently running in over 500 Living Well community pharmacies and promotes uptake of the flu, COVID-19, RSV and pneumococcal vaccines, for those who are eligible. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any epidemiological modelling his Department has undertaken on the risk of rising flu levels this winter.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency (PHA) monitors a number of indicators of the incidence and severity of influenza and other respiratory infections and reports many key indicators on the PHA website weekly during the winter season.  PHA also monitors information from elsewhere in the world about hazards to health, including seasonal influenza, and publishes a high-level summary on its website every week.  The intelligence from these reporting processes is also communicated directly to my officials. PHA uses a quasi-Poisson statistical model on a large number of surveillance indicators to detect outbreaks and unusual infectious disease activity. This detected the early start to the influenza season, contributing to PHA’s advice to my Department that influenza was circulating earlier than usual.  As a result, the Chief Medical Officer issued guidance to HSC on 23 September 2025 that antivirals could be prescribed for suspected influenza in the community.The seasonal Influenza A epidemic has waned rapidly since reaching its peak before Christmas. The peak number of admissions each week was similar to last year, though more of those affected were children.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assessment he has made on the capacity of the health and social care system to manage the combined pressures of rising flu cases and other winter demands.
answer_text: When we published the Department’s Overarching Winter Plan alongside the Trusts’ local plans in October, I stressed that the system and the public need to work together and all play our part. This will help to make sure that HSC services are ready and available for those who need them most. Improving effectiveness includes measures such as getting vaccinated, taking steps to stay well and co-operating with the hospital discharge processes.The 2025/26 Winter Plan builds on last year’s foundations, focusing on expanding urgent care alternatives such as Phone First, minor injury units, and urgent care centres. These initiatives are embedded within each Trust’s Plans. In advance of Christmas, all Trusts focused on increased discharges to maximise bed capacity, putting hospital sites in an improved position compared to the same period in 2024. While Emergency Department (ED) attendances and ambulance arrivals were on some days higher than forecast, we have seen improvement in comparison with 2024.  While Flu rates peaked earlier than last year, I am encouraged that related admissions had begun to decrease before Christmas, in all age groups.  Flu-related hospital admissions stabilised, remaining lower than 2024 and ICU admissions rose slightly but remained below 2024 levels. We must however remain vigilant and continue to take all the steps we can to reduce the transmission of infectious diseases.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are any plans to record and publish the specific data required for the standardised mortality ratio in order to ascertain the number of excess deaths associated with long waits in Emergency Departments.
answer_text: I refer to my previous response to AQW 33053/ 22-27 on this matter.  I acknowledge the serious concerns regarding prolonged waiting times in emergency departments (EDs) and remain committed to working with Health and Social Care Trusts and community services to implement a range of actions aimed at improving patient flow and reducing delays for patients. My Department continues to work closely with the Royal College of Emergency Medicine (RCEM) and other professional bodies to identify and apply best practice approaches to improve flow within hospitals.Through engagement with the Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) Emergency Medicine Team, my Department is aware of the Standard Mortality Ratio methodology, which estimates excess deaths associated with stays of 12 hours or longer prior to admission to a hospital bed. However, my Department does not hold specific data on the number of excess deaths linked to extended waits in EDs. Establishing causality in such cases is highly complex and would require more granular data than is currently available.Recognising the significant challenges faced by EDs, my Department has worked closely with Health and Social Care Trusts and community services to implement a range of measures aimed at improving patient flow and reducing delays.To help alleviate pressures within EDs, my Department has introduced a number of targeted initiatives. These include the expansion of urgent care centres, the roll-out of Phone First services and the development of minor injury units. These services provide patients with timely alternatives to ED attendance, ensuring that those who do attend can be seen, treated and discharged more promptly.Additional support has also been provided to Trusts ahead of winter to address ambulance handover delays outside EDs, ensuring that patients are transferred safely and without unnecessary delay. Furthermore, in preparation for the winter period, my Department has worked with the RCEM and the Trusts to secure the appointment of an additional 20 emergency medicine consultants over the past year. This will strengthen resilience and enhance senior decision-making capacity within EDs.All of these measures are designed to improve patient flow throughout hospitals, reduce congestion in EDs and improve waiting times. These actions form part of a broader, system-wide approach to ensure that patients receive timely, safe and effective care both within EDs and across the wider health and social care system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how his Department can support people experiencing homelessness, given the findings of the Simon Community’s October 2025 report Childhood Adversity and Homelessness in Northern Ireland: Breaking the Cycle, which shows that diagnosed mental health conditions are around four times higher for single adults living in homeless hostels than the general population.
answer_text: My Department is committed to understanding and responding to the healthcare needs of those experiencing homelessness in Northern Ireland.Those experiencing chronic homelessness can have diverse needs, mental and physical health issues, addictions, legal and justice issues. The individuals often require very pronounced, integrated and specialist support needs to facilitate an exit from homelessness. We are committed to tackling homelessness, and the issues associated with this, through a variety of workstreams and strategies including the Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery Substance Use Strategy, Mental Health Strategy and continued cross departmental collaboration with DfC and DoJ and sector engagement with key stakeholders. My Department continues to lead on a number of actions to tackle homelessness from a non-accommodation perspective via the Interdepartmental Homelessness Action Plan and contributes to the Housing Supply Strategy.My officials are currently in the process of initiating a Health Homelessness Policy Advisory Group, bringing together key policy officials across the Department, and relevant ALBs to foster a high level of internal collaboration across the Department in regard to homelessness policy, improve joined up thinking with the aim of improving overall collaboration cross-Departmentally and delivering improved outcomes for those experiencing homelessness.The Complex Lives initiative established by the Public Health Agency (PHA), Belfast City Council, and the Northern Ireland Housing Executive, is operating and providing support to those who are homeless and at risk in the Belfast area. With a Multi-Disciplinary Team in place, it is providing a whole system, accountable care approach to supporting people experiencing multiple disadvantages with the aim of helping people engage, stabilise, and find a new way forward with their lives. Further support includes the Belfast Inclusion Health Services hub, funded by the PHA, which provides multi-disciplinary community care, including dental, podiatry, mental health, and social work services. The Belfast Inclusion Health Service is a nurse-led multidisciplinary service set up to address the health and social care needs of those experiencing homelessness within the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust area. Further inclusion nurses are also in place in all Trust areas, to support the needs of those experiencing homelessness.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given that the Urgent and Emergency Care Waiting Time Statistics for Northern Ireland (April - June 2025) had over 500 patients waiting over two days in Emergency Departments (ED) for a bed on the ward and that the Royal College of Emergency Medicine has published evidence of the mortality risk for patients waiting for beds for a long time in overcrowded EDs; whether he will consider eradicating 24 and 48 hour waits in EDs.
answer_text: I acknowledge that the current waiting times in our Emergency Departments are too long for patients across Northern Ireland. This situation has arisen due to several factors, including funding that falls short of objectively assessed need and previous missed opportunities to reform health and social care delivery. The pressures are related not just to the numbers of patients attending ED, but also the acuity of patient conditions. A central factor is capacity in social care, which impacts on hospital discharge rates and consequently on patient flow from EDs.This is why my long-term vision involves diverting patients to the most appropriate care pathways first time, to provide patients with the best care possible and to avoid unnecessary ED attendance. This includes an increase in the number of same day emergency care clinics and an increased focus on community provision, through services such as Hospital at Home.   There is no quick fix, but I remain committed to addressing the current situation subject to sustained long-term effort, additional recurrent funding and support across the political spectrum.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline any measures his Department is putting in place to ensure that beds are available for patients who need to be admitted to hospital from Emergency Departments (ED) instead of having to wait in the ED.
answer_text: As part of the GIRFT Review, a Summary Emergency Department Indicator Table (SEDIT) was developed for Northern Ireland, presenting data across four key areas: demand, capacity, flow, and outcomes. In assessing capacity, consideration is given to factors such as the number of resus and majors treatment cubicles in Emergency Departments (Eds), the availability of ED consultant and nursing staff, as well as acute and elderly beds available in each hospital. The SEDIT shows that for the majority of sites in Northern Ireland, when compared to peers in England and Wales, our hospitals have sufficient capacity to manage the demand levels. Where our hospitals do not compare favourably with peers is around flow and outcomes. This indicates that hospital capacity is not the limiting factor, but rather flow through our hospitals, highlighting the need to improve discharge processes and overall patient flow.In order to help Trusts manage pressures over the winter period, £10m has been re-purposed and made available to Trusts for targeted actions across a range of areas from pre-hospital care through to acute care and into the community. These are to be in place by the end of October and form part of the Trusts unscheduled care plans. The measures across Trusts include increased capacity of Hospital at Home in Belfast, South Eastern and Northern Trusts, which provides extra bed capacity in the community; eight additional short stay inpatient beds at Altnagelvin along with 12 additional intermediate care beds; frailty assessment at the front door of acute hospitals; expansion of ambulatory and Same Day Emergency Care (SDEC); multidisciplinary team assessment at the front door of acute hospitals; and enhanced capacity for early review teams and discharge co-ordination in Trusts.This additional capacity supplements hospital inpatient beds and aims to avoid admission and provide alternative pathways for patients which are often more appropriate than additional inpatient beds. At times of increased pressure, all hospitals have capacity to open additional inpatient escalation beds. In total across our hospitals, an additional 258 beds can be opened to help manage demand.To address back door pressures and accelerate hospital discharges, a range of initiatives are being implemented. These include reviewing the Regional Care Home Contract to better support individuals with complex needs, expanding home care capacity using technology and AI, and developing a regional model for service delivery. The Early Review Team reassesses care packages within eight weeks to recycle hours back into the system, while the Care Line Live digital tool helps Trusts optimise scheduling and staffing. The Trusted Assessor model is improving transitions from hospital to care homes, with plans to expand to a seven-day service. A regional digital app is also being developed to provide daily updates on care home bed availability, and direct payments including emergency options are being promot

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether an assessment has been made of the Royal College of Emergency Medicine's September 2025 press release on excess deaths associated with long waits in Scotland’s Emergency Departments before admission in 2024; and (ii) to detail the number of similarly associated excess deaths in Northern Ireland using the Standard Mortality Ratio, in 2024.
answer_text: I recognise the serious concerns raised by prolonged waiting times in our Emergency Departments and continue to work with Trusts and community services to implement a range of actions aimed at improving patient flow and reducing delays for patients. My Department works closely with the Royal College of Emergency Medicine and other professional bodies to look at how best we can improve flow in our hospitals.Through work with the GIRFT Emergency Medicine Team, my Department is aware of the Standard Mortality Ratio methodology which estimates excess deaths related to stays of 12-hours or longer before being admitted to a hospital bed. However, My Department does not hold specific data on the number of excess deaths associated with long waits in Emergency Departments. Determining causality in such cases is complex and not possible due to limitations in current data collection systems.In 2025/26, I am determined to find better ways to work as a system to tackle waiting times in EDs. That is why I initiated a series of winter planning workshops to identify solutions to the system flow issues that we collectively face. The workshops saw attendance from HSC leaders across the system, including Royal Colleges, clinicians, and other frontline services. I hope to publish my Winter Preparedness Plan imminently.  The plan will identify other steps that my Department and key partners are taking to help manage pressures.Ultimately, all of these measures are aimed at improving flow throughout the hospital, reducing congestion in our EDs and improving waiting times.These efforts are part of a broader system-wide approach to ensure patients receive timely, safe, and effective care, both within EDs and across the wider health and social care system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail when his Department intends to introduce regular GP health checks as is the case in England, to enhance general health and enable early intervention as part of planning to take pressure off Emergency Departments.
To ask the Minister of Health to outline what differences will be apparent in this year’s Winter Preparedness Plan compared to that published on 6 November 2024.
answer_text: Earlier this year, under the leadership of the Chief Medical Officer and Chief Nursing Officer, I brought together senior leaders from across the Health and Social Care system to explore the learning from last winter, as we plan for winter 2025/26. I was clear that I wanted a whole system approach looking at what could be achieved both in advance of this winter and in the longer-term.This process identified the need for a particular focus on interventions to improve the care of those older people living with frailty and other co-morbidities, who have been identified as some of the most vulnerable to the risks associated with the winter period, and this will be reflected in this year’s plan. Seven action plans have been established which focus on reducing hospital demand by supporting older people to stay well and receive appropriate care in the community. This includes improving the use of advance clinical care planning and enhancing pathways for patients, ensuring the right care is delivered in the right place, first time.Of course, like in previous years, this year’s Winter Preparedness Plan will also identify other steps that the Department and key partners are taking to help manage pressures, including enhancements for General Practice, efforts to tackle ambulance handover delays and additions to Community Pharmacy services. We have also mobilised an extensive vaccination programme, to minimise the impact from winter infections such as influenza, RSV and COVID-19.I will publish the Winter Preparedness Plan imminently and, as in previous years, we will again call upon the public to do everything possible to help protect our system, including utilising community services, where available, and getting vaccinated, if eligible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how his Department assesses the number of excess deaths caused by excessive waits in Emergency Departments.
answer_text: My Department does not hold specific data on the number of excess deaths directly attributable to excessive waits in emergency departments (EDs) during the periods in question. Determining causality in such cases is complex and not possible due to limitations in current data collection systems. However, I recognise the serious concerns raised by prolonged waiting times and continue to work with Trusts and community services to implement a range of actions aimed at improving patient flow and reducing delays. To help alleviate pressures in EDs, my Department has implemented a range of targeted initiatives. These include the expansion of Urgent Care Centres, Phone First Services and Minor Injury Units, offering patients timely alternatives to ED attendance.Ahead of the coming winter, the department has worked with the Royal College of Emergency Medicine (RCEM) and the Trusts to ensure that an additional 18 emergency medicine consultants have been appointed to work in EDs across Northern Ireland. A further ten consultants are expected to complete their training by December 2025, and it is anticipated that they will also be recruited locally to provide further resilience and senior decision-making capacity to our EDs this winter.In 2025/26, I am determined to find better ways to work as a system to tackle waiting times in EDs. That is why I initiated a series of winter planning workshops to identify solutions to the system flow issues that we collectively face. The workshops saw attendance from HSC leaders across the system, including Royal Colleges, clinicians, and other frontline services. I hope to publish my Winter Preparedness Plan in the coming weeks that will aim to see improved outcomes before the next winter.  The plan will identify other steps that my Department and key partners are taking to help manage pressures.Ultimately, all of these measures are aimed at improving flow throughout the hospital, reducing congestion in our EDs and improving waiting times.These efforts are part of a broader system-wide approach to ensure patients receive timely, safe, and effective care, both within EDs and across the wider health and social care system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action he is taking to overcome winter vaccination programme hesitancy.
answer_text: Whilst over 800,000 COVID-19 and influenza vaccinations were administered as part of the 2024/25 programmes, uptake of both vaccinations has declined in some eligible cohorts in recent years. Reasons behind the decline can be influenced by multiple factors including broader socio-cultural, trust (or distrust) in healthcare systems and providers, past experiences with vaccines or healthcare encounters, perceived risks of diseases versus vaccine side effects, recommendations from healthcare institutions, and personal beliefs, as well as access to locations to receive vaccination.In addressing this complex issue, the Public Health Agency (PHA) has established a multi-disciplinary working group to identify and implement how to improve vaccination uptake rates.  An action plan has been developed to support the work of the group across five separate themes of data; access to services; communications; operational management; and training and education. Vaccination is now available from multiple sources right across NI, such as all GP practices, participating community pharmacies and Trust led vaccination clinics and I would encourage anyone eligible to take up the offer of vaccination when invited to do so.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the impact of excluding people younger than 75 years old from the commencement of the flu vaccination programme eligible cohort.
answer_text: Eligible cohorts for the autumn and winter 2025 influenza vaccination programme in Northern Ireland are based on advice of the Joint Committee on Vaccination and Immunisation (JCVI).Based on this advice, the following cohorts are eligible for the current Northern Ireland influenza vaccination programme:all those aged 65 years and over on 31 March 2026,all those aged 18 years to under 65 years in clinical risk groups, those in long-stay residential care homes,pregnant women,all preschool children aged two to four years on 1 September 2025,all school-aged children (up to and including year 12),all children in clinical risk groups aged from 6 months to less than 18 years, carers,close contacts of immunocompromised individuals,high risk poultry and avian animal health workers, andall health and social care workers The JCVI keeps its advice on seasonal flu vaccination programmes under regular review and publishes this on an annual basis. Should their advice relating to eligible cohorts for future programmes change, my Department will consider their updated recommendations at that time.

answer_text: Eligible cohorts for the autumn and winter 2025 influenza vaccination programme in Northern Ireland are based on advice of the Joint Committee on Vaccination and Immunisation (JCVI).Based on this advice, the following cohorts are eligible for the current Northern Ireland influenza vaccination programme:all those aged 65 years and over on 31 March 2026,all those aged 18 years to under 65 years in clinical risk groups, those in long-stay residential care homes,pregnant women,all preschool children aged two to four years on 1 September 2025,all school-aged children (up to and including year 12),all children in clinical risk groups aged from 6 months to less than 18 years, carers,close contacts of immunocompromised individuals,high risk poultry and avian animal health workers, andall health and social care workers The JCVI keeps its advice on seasonal flu vaccination programmes under regular review and publishes this on an annual basis. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline any representations he has made to the Joint Committee on Vaccination and Immunisation on the inclusion of the 50-64 age cohort from the commencement of the winter flu vaccination programme in 2025.
answer_text: Eligible cohorts for the autumn and winter 2025-26 influenza vaccination programme in Northern Ireland are based on the advice of the Joint Committee on Vaccination and Immunisation (JCVI).  In making its advice, the JCVI conducts a thorough review of the latest data including vaccine effectiveness data from UK influenza disease surveillance to inform expert recommendations.The influenza vaccination programme aims to provide direct protection to those who JCVI consider are at higher risk of flu-associated morbidity and mortality, and to reduce transmission to all age groups through the vaccination of children. Whilst JCVI does not recommend influenza vaccination for those aged 50 to 64 years old on a population basis, people aged 50 to 64 years old would be eligible for vaccination if they belong to any of the following groups:Those who are in a clinical risk group,Close contacts of immunocompromised individuals,Carers,Those who reside in a residential care home, Health and social care workers, andHigh risk poultry and avian animal health workersThe JCVI keeps its advice on seasonal flu vaccination programmes under regular review and publishes this on an annual basis. My Department will consider any updated advice made by JCVI relating to eligible cohorts for future programmes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQO 2406/22-27, whether a value for money assessment has been carried out on the £2,251 spent on commissioning and producing the dossier entitled, The Family Footsteps of JD Vance, in light of reports that the researcher carrying out the project had told his Department that no definitive link could be found.
answer_text: A value for money assessment has not been carried out. This research contributes to my Department’s wider priorities to mark the 250th anniversary of the creation of the United States following the printing of the Declaration of Independence in July 1776. This is an important milestone and presents a significant opportunity to build on the historical and cultural connections that exist between Northern Ireland and the US.Settlers from Ulster made an invaluable contribution to the creation of the United States, and the semiquincentennial offers the opportunity to highlight key individuals from here such as Charles Thomson, and John Dunlap who played such seminal roles. My engagement with key US cultural institutions has already yielded valuable exchanges to date and I am eager to explore further opportunities. I have agreed a Memorandum of Understanding with the America 250 Commission reflecting the commitment to sustaining and expanding these cultural ties. I was pleased to receive a letter from Vice President JD Vance expressing his appreciation for the book about his family ancestry.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans he has to expand training opportunities for healthcare professionals in migraine diagnosis and management.
answer_text: In the context of ongoing capacity constraints within neurology services, the final report of my Department’s Regional Review of Neurology Services proposes a range of recommendations to support the growth and development of the neurology workforce. The report recommends significant growth across the Consultant, Nursing and Allied Health Professionals (AHP) workforce. In addition to this growth, the report also recommends the development of a broader skill mix through the development and growth of extended roles such as GPs with Enhanced Roles, Advanced Nurse Practitioners, Consultant AHP roles and Specialty and Specialist Doctors. This will provide increased opportunities for healthcare professionals to develop skills in the diagnosis and management of neurological conditions including migraine. More widely, the report recommends increased exposure in Neurology for medical trainees to generate interest in the specialty and maximise recruitment to the Neurology Training Programme. It also proposes a specific recommendation for training in Neurology for non-Neurology hospital specialists and across the wider HSC workforce to support the management of people with neurological conditions including migraine. My officials are currently undertaking an analysis of responses to the recent public consultation on the final report of the review to inform next steps. However, I must be clear that implementation of the report’s recommendations will require significant additional funding.  While I am committed to carefully considering the need for additional funding, the scale and pace of implementation may have to be adjusted in line with the funding available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail when he will introduce a mandatory reporting duty for health professionals regarding female genital mutilation, in line with England and Wales.
answer_text: Currently my department has no plans to introduce a mandatory reporting duty for health professionals regarding Female Genital Mutilation (FGM), in line with England and Wales.Healthcare professionals in Northern Ireland are already bound by both legal and professional obligations to report abuse. FGM is a serious criminal offence. Under section 5 of the Criminal Law Act (Northern Ireland) 1967, any person who knows or believes that a relevant offence has been committed and has information likely to assist in the apprehension, prosecution or conviction of the offender is legally required to disclose that information to police. Failure to do so, without reasonable excuse, constitutes a criminal offence. In addition to this legal duty, health professionals must adhere to safeguarding policies and procedures such as “Multi-agency Practice Guidelines - Female Genital Mutilation” (accessible via https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/multi-agency-practice-guidelines-female-genital-mutilation-health) and the SBNI’s Regional Core Child Protection Policy and Procedures (accessible via https://www.proceduresonline.com/sbni ). These guidelines emphasise the duty to identify, assess, and respond to risk, including making referrals to social services or the PSNI where appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has carried out any assessment of the impact on workforce retention and patient outcomes in relation to the lack of progression from band 5 to band 6 for many registered nurses, compared to colleagues in other healthcare professions.
To ask the Minister of Education whether he will take forward recommendations contained within the Independent Review of Education in relation to the creation of jointly managed community schools.
answer_text: My Department has already published a Circular, in place since 2015, setting out the framework for jointly managed schools, and this remains available on the Department’s website. To date, no sustainable proposal for a jointly managed solution has been brought forward under that framework. As Minister, I have outlined in the published response to the Independent Review of Education, that my Department will develop further guidance and support to facilitate schools from different sectors to become Jointly Managed Community Schools. Such guidance will build upon existing support; and reinforce that any future decisions are informed by evidence, shaped by local need, and centred on the best interests of children and young people. However, it is not an immediate priority, in the context of current demand and other educational challenges.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the steps his Department took to verify the accuracy of genealogical and DNA claims before commissioning and producing the dossier entitled, The Family Footsteps of JD Vance.
answer_text: I asked my officials as part of the wider America250 commemorations to undertake research to explore genealogical links between Vice President JD Vance and his Ulster-Scots ancestry. In his book, Hillbilly Elegy, the Vice President references his Scots Irish heritage.  The Public Record Office commissioned an experienced independent researcher in the United States to work back through primary sources in the USA.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any discussions she has had with the Minister of Finance in relation to town centre regeneration.
answer_text: In March 2022 The Executive Office published its Report with recommendations on the High Street Task Force. My Department is responsible for recommendation 8 of the Report: ‘Fair Work on Our High Streets’. This recommendation aligns with my economic priority on Good Jobs. Work is ongoing within my Department to progress this recommendation and this includes the development of a new Employment Rights Bill, promotion of greater adoption of the Real Living Wage, and collaboration with the Labour Relations Agency to create a Good Employment Charter. This work will have a positive impact across town centres in the North.My officials are also working with all eleven Councils to develop Local Economic Partnerships to address economic need on a sub-regional scale.The Department of Finance provides support to businesses each year through a number of rate interventions. In response to feedback from business representatives and stakeholders, a strategic review of the Small Business Rate Relief and Non-Domestic Vacant Rating schemes is at an advanced stage.As town centre regeneration is a complex issue which cuts across many government departments and other public bodies and agencies, I remain fully committed to working collaboratively with my Executive colleagues to identify and deliver effective, sustainable solutions.to identify and deliver effective, sustainable solutions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how his Department plans to support mature applicants and those changing career into the social work profession, in order to address persistent staff shortages across the Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: The Department of Health provides an Incentive Scheme for student social workers who are domicile and studying full-time in Northern Ireland. A key objective of the Scheme is to attract more mature students by meeting some of the costs associated with full-time study. Applicants can access a non-means tested allowance of £4,000 for each year of their study. They are also awarded £500 per year to cover expenses whilst on Practice Learning placements. Student Incentive Scheme payments are in addition to any student finance support, which can be applied for separately.Since 2020 the Department has also been commissioning training places for social workers with the Open University NI. This new route into social work has proved very attractive to more mature and experienced social care workers already in employment with HSC Trusts as it allows them to remain in post while they study. To date, more than one hundred social care workers from HSC Trusts have availed of the Open University route.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will publish the findings of the ADHD review.
answer_text: The ADHD needs assessment is not yet complete.  The initial estimated completion date was end June 2025 and considerable work has already been progressed. However, due to a desire and demand for further stakeholder engagement, information gathering and analysis which will ensure a robust evidence base and proposals, it is likely that the work will now be completed by September 2025.    A decision on publication of the needs assessment will be taken once the final report has been received and considered by the Department.
