question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) the current capability of encompass to produce clinical, operational and financial audit reports; and (ii) what additional financial investment or system development would be required to ensure encompass can fully support comprehensive audit and assurance functions across the health service.
answer_text: The encompass system is a key component of Northern Ireland’s digital health transformation, aimed at improving clinical, operational, and financial oversight across the health and social care service. In addition to the data and tools for analytics in support of comprehensive audit within encompass, the Northern Ireland Health Analytics Platform (NIHAP) and the Patient Level Information Costing System (PLICS) continue to develop analytical capabilities, bringing together datasets that are not in encompass, alongside encompass data.While encompass provides a strong foundation for audit, development is ongoing in partnership with HSC Trust, DoH-SPPG and PHA partners to introduce additional clinical Key Performance Indicators and reporting and assurance capabilities. Additional training for staff and managers to support them in the best use of the data and tools available in encompass is ongoing, now all HSC Trusts are live and stabilising.Additional investment may be required in future to avail of technological advances and fully leverage AI, this will be subject to additional business case and approval processes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer (i) AQW 26608/22-27; and (ii) AQW 27005/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail whether there are plans to have fully joined-up electronic patient records with an encompass system roll-out across primary care services.
answer_text: The encompass system has now been successfully implemented across all five HSCNI Trusts, supporting secondary, social, and community care services. Further extension of its Electronic Patient Record (EPR) to include primary care, however, presents a number of technical, contractual, financial and information governance challenges.Most General Practitioners (GPs) operate within HSCNI as independent contractors and retain autonomy over their Practice Management Systems and other software used within their practices. The "GP System of Choice” (GPSoC) is part of the General Medical Services (GMS) contract with the Department of Health, ensuring that all systems meet NHS accreditation standards while providing Practices with choice. A full roll-out of encompass across multiple primary care providers therefore presents a number of additional challenges including, but not limited to, alignment of technology and data migration/stewardship. In addition, the encompass EPR supplier, Epic, does not currently provide a traditional UK Practice Management System as per GPSoC requirements. Integration with existing primary care systems has however been a key consideration for encompass and GP representatives across the region have actively contributed to its design and implementation. All practices now benefit from read-only access to the EPR through a platform called EpicCare Link. This provides a role-appropriate view of the patient record and facilitates GPs to make referrals, order laboratory tests and imaging, and communicate securely with HSC colleagues.Provision has also been made to develop further urgent and ambulatory care functionality to support Northern Ireland’s primary care Out of Hours (OOHs) service providers. This will provide the integrated technical foundation for new regional care models for urgent and emergency care across HSCNI. Additionally, ongoing configuration of encompass to deliver primary care functionality in the prison health setting within South Eastern HSC Trust may provide valuable insight and learning to inform future decision making. At present however, there are no plans for a wider roll-out of the encompass system across primary care services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the estimated cost of delivering the gonorrhoea vaccination programme; (ii) the estimated cost to the health service of treating gonorrhoea cases, including cases of antibiotic resistance over the same period; and (iii) the rationale for proceeding with the programme given the reported vaccine efficacy of up to 40 per cent.
answer_text: The estimated cost of delivering the gonorrhoea vaccination programme in Northern Ireland is approximately £100,000.Estimating the overall cost of treating gonorrhoea and its complications is complex, involving direct medical costs, lost productivity, and long-term healthcare needs. There were 1,208 cases of gonorrhoea recorded at sexual health clinics in Northern Ireland in 2024 which would have incurred costs for testing, treating, and tracking these infections. Untreated gonorrhoea can lead to serious health complications such as pelvic inflammatory disease, infertility, and an increased risk of HIV transmission, which require more intensive and expensive medical interventions. The rise of drug-resistant strains of gonorrhoea also adds another layer of complexity to disease management and cost estimations. The British Association of Sexual health and HIV has estimated that the cost of treating an antibiotic-resistant gonorrhoea infection is around six times higher than a standard gonorrhoea infection.My Department follows the advice of the Joint Committee on Vaccination and Immunisation (JCVI), an independent expert scientific advisory committee which advises the UK health departments on vaccination and immunisation matters.   JCVI considered the evidence presented in terms of programme cost-effectiveness and likely impact on gonorrhoea epidemiology.  Based on this evidence JCVI recommended that a targeted programme should be initiated using the 4CMenB vaccine for the prevention of gonorrhoea in those who are considered to be at greatest risk of infection.  In Northern Ireland the vaccine will be offered to those considered to be at greatest risk, while they are attending Genito-urinary Medicine (GUM) clinics.  Vaccination is expected to help reduce the chance of infection in those vaccinated as well as prevention of onward transmission, which in turn will reduce the use of antibiotics and reduce the potential future burden of antimicrobial resistance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given that Northern Ireland remains the only UK region without publicly funded bariatric surgery, whether patients referred elsewhere in the UK for surgery are able to access adequate and safe postoperative care and long-term follow-up within Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Work is currently being taken forward by my Department to develop a Regional Obesity Management service specification.  The aim of service is to provide support to individuals who are struggling with obesity or being overweight, with access criteria developed for the service. In its first phase, which is expected to commence early 2026, the focus will be on the provision of medical injections for weight loss, with associated lifestyle support to ensure maximum effectiveness. As work progresses, there may be further phases to this service development to include other interventions, such as bariatric (weight loss) surgery, including postoperative care and long-term follow-up, subject to funding. Whilst bariatric surgery is not currently available in NI, it may be possible for patients to be transferred to other parts of the UK for surgery via the Extra Contractual Referral scheme, subject to clinical exceptionality. Under this scheme, patients may be entitled to appropriate post-operative support in line with clinical guidelines.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given that future phases, including bariatric surgery, will be subject to funding within the Regional Obesity Management Service, to outline what treatment options are currently available for patients who have been clinically recommended for weight loss surgery.
answer_text: Work is currently being taken forward by my Department to develop a Regional Obesity Management service specification.  In its first phase, which is expected to commence early 2026, the focus will be on the provision of medical injections for weight loss, with associated lifestyle support to ensure maximum effectiveness. As work progresses, there may be further phases to this service development to include other interventions, such as bariatric (weight loss) surgery, subject to funding. With regard to alternative options for surgical treatment, under the Extra Contractual Referral scheme, it may be possible for an individual to access healthcare assessment and/or surgical treatment outside of NI.  This scheme is available for patients who demonstrate clear clinical exceptionality and would require the patient being transferred to a service provider on the UK mainland.  Body contouring procedures are commissioned in Northern Ireland for patients who meet the Effective Use of Resources Policy criteria for treatment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, following the March 2025 NI Audit Office report Health and Social Care Imaging Services, what sites are being considered for the expansion of imaging services.
To ask the Minister of Health whether he intends to prioritise imaging services in the capital budget.

To ask the Minister of Health, in light of the March 2025 NI Audit Office report Health and Social Care Imaging Services, how he is going to ensure increased availability of CT scans, given that timely access to these is crucial for stroke treatments.
answer_text: The recently published Elective Care Framework Implementation and Funding Plan sets out clear proposals for how we will systematically tackle the backlog of patients waiting longer than Ministerial standards and how we will transform services to allow us to meet the population’s demands in the future.As part of the Implementation and Funding Plan, £12m has been secured to expand diagnostic imaging capacity across the region.  This not only includes increasing capacity for CT but will include areas such as MRI and ultrasound.  The initial focus will be ensuring that scanning sessions, not already commissioned, are recurrently funded Monday to Friday. Further work will then be undertaken to look at evening and weekend working.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether a final decision has been made in relation to remedial works to address the contamination issues in the water treatment system at the new Maternity Hospital.

To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) what funding is required to deliver the Community Pharmacy Strategic Plan 2030; (ii) what business cases including transformational bids, he has submitted to secure this funding; and (iii) the timescale for delivery of this Plan.
answer_text: The Community Pharmacy Strategic Plan brings together four existing inter-related programmes of work led by the Department, to support the delivery of six strategic priorities up to 2030. Those four work programmes relate to: Service Development; Legislative Reform; Workforce; and ePharmacy. Some aspects of these work programmes are already in train and funded, however full delivery of the strategic vision is subject to additional funding and implementation will be phased over the lifetime of the strategy. Additional requirements of up to £15m have been identified which, if secured, will support the development of community pharmacy services in line with the commissioning priorities outlined in the 2025-28 commissioning plan for community pharmacy services, which the Department and Community Pharmacy NI are currently working on.In addition, the Department’s ePharmacy Digital Reform Programme for Primary Care comprises of four related projects:		Go Pharmacy NI; 		Electronic Transmission of Prescriptions;		Electronic Prescribing Eligibility System (EPES) replacement; and		NI Drug Tariff Masterfile.		The indicative timescale for delivery of the ePharmacy programme is by September 2032, subject to funding, and work is currently being undertaken to develop the relevant business cases. An outline business case for the Go Pharmacy project is expected to be completed in July 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service’s Community Resuscitation Strategy; and (iI) whether a review is currently being undertaken to inform the development of a successor strategy.
answer_text: The Community Resuscitation Strategy was launched in July 2014 by the then Minister for Health, Edwin Poots, with the aim to increase the survival rate for those who suffer an out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, to the highest level that can be achieved across Northern Ireland.  The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) is leading on the successful implementation of the Community Resuscitation Strategy and is funded to provide a supporting role in advising organisations, businesses, and individuals regarding siting of defibrillators and best practice in the use of defibrillators. Public engagement and awareness sessions on CPR and use of AEDs in communities, continue to increase in number year on year. Community resuscitation strategy groups within individual council areas, develop specific objectives and action plans to improve survivability from out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, with the aid of local resources. NIAS has worked with the British Heart Foundation to develop ‘The Circuit’ (https://www.thecircuit.uk/) which is a national online defibrillator network linking to every ambulance service in the UK.  NIAS can readily provide information on AEDs registered on ‘The Circuit’ to 999 callers, should they require access.There are currently 3,888 Automated External Defibrillators (AEDs) available to the public and registered through NIAS on ‘The Circuit’.  NIAS Community Resuscitation Team continue to be involved in activities within communities which will strengthen the chain of survival, and NIAS have installed 35 publicly accessible defibrillators outside NIAS premises across the province.There are currently 426 Community First Responder (CFR) volunteers and 24 schemes in NI.  NI also has approximately 2,500 members of the public who have a minimum of Basic Life Support training and have registered to be a GoodSAM responder. Since members of the public went live on GoodSAM in February 2023 over 297 people have accepted a GoodSAM alert to a cardiac arrest.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what procedures are followed for the disposal or repurposing of decommissioned diagnostic scanners.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts have waste and asset management policies and procedures in place so that assets no longer required are disposed of in a safe and appropriate manner in accordance with the relevant legislation and best practice. Diagnostic scanners may be deemed no longer required if surplus to requirements, obsolete, beyond economic repair or at the end of their useful life. If the scanner is beyond repair or use, it must be disposed of in compliance with the Waste Electrical and Electronic Equipment (WEEE) Regulations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether patients will be able to access treatment under the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme from independent sector providers based within Northern Ireland.
To ask the Minister of Health to outline his Department's policies and protocols regarding the decommissioning of diagnostic imaging scanners, including MRI, CT, X-ray, and ultrasound equipment.
answer_text: My Department established the Regional Medical Imaging Board to oversee the recommendations arising from the Strategic Framework for Imaging Services in Health and Social Care, 2018. The Board monitors the use, configuration and age of imaging equipment across all imaging services in Health and Social Care and has developed a system for prioritising the replacement of equipment and the procurement of additional equipment in line with population needs and available budget.Decommissioning of aged equipment is undertaken by each Health and Social Trust in accordance with their local governance processes, assisted by expert advice from medical physics.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has explored options to extend the operational life of diagnostic scanners beyond their standard lifespan where safe and clinically appropriate, in order to boost provision of service.
answer_text: My Department’s Regional Medical Imaging Board monitors the use, configuration and age of all imaging equipment across imaging services in Health and Social Care and has developed a system for prioritising the replacement of equipment and the procurement of additional equipment in line with population needs and available budget. Each Trust manages aged equipment in accordance with its local governance processes and is assisted by expert advice from medical physics colleagues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) whether diagnostic scanners are routinely replaced following decommissioning or obsolescence; and (ii) what mechanisms are in place to ensure continuity of service.
answer_text: I can confirm that the vast majority of scanners are routinely replaced following decommissioning or obsolescence and would refer you to my response to AQW 27003/22-27 which includes full breakdown of various scanners replaced within each Health and Social Care Trust over the last 10 years.Continuity of service is managed by each Trust in accordance with local governance processes. Items of equipment which are forecast to affect service continuity can be brought to the attention of the Regional Medical Imaging Board’s Capital Equipment Sub-group, which has a system in place for prioritising the replacement of equipment and the procurement of additional equipment in line with population needs and available budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he expects scoping work on a lung screening programme to be completed and published.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps he is taking to reduce cancer inequalities, in light of the Cancer Research UK February 2025 report entitled UK Cancer Mortality by Deprivation that found cancer death rates are around 45 per cent higher for people living in the most deprived areas of Northern Ireland compared to the least deprived with around 630 extra cancer deaths in Northern Ireland each year linked to socioeconomic inequality.
answer_text: I welcome the findings in the Cancer Research UK (CRUK) report entitled Cancer in the UK 2025: Socioeconomic deprivation. These findings reinforce my commitment to tackle health inequalities.The report, quite rightly, identifies a number of factors such as increased obesity and smoking incidence, lower participation in screening programmes, and reduced awareness of the signs and symptoms of cancer as the leading cause of increased incidence and mortality and poorer outcomes for cancer patients.Officials in my Department are working across departmental boundaries and with local government and community and voluntary sector partners to ensure that those in the most deprived communities can access health and social care services effectively.  This approach is a key step to reducing health inequalities and improving overall the health and wellbeing of people across these deprived communities.  My Department’s “Making Life Better” strategy provides an overarching strategic framework for public health through which the Northern Ireland Executive committed to creating the conditions for individuals, families and communities to take greater control over their lives and be enabled to lead healthy lives.In addition, the Northern Ireland Cancer Strategy that launched in March 2022 has a range of strategic themes and high-level actions that will deal with cancer inequalities through service transformation and service optimisation to ensure that there is both equity of access to cancer services and improved patient outcomes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the timeline for introducing Minimum Unit Pricing for alcohol.
answer_text: As I have made clear, I believe that Minimum Unit Pricing for Alcohol could be a key mechanism to reduce alcohol-related harm in Northern Ireland and to address the health inequalities that exist in this area. I previously indicated that I hoped to bring legislation to the Assembly in the final year of the mandate, however this is predicated on obtaining Executive agreement. I continue to await formal confirmation of the position of all my Executive colleagues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will publish a strategy for addressing obesity.
answer_text: My Department’s ‘A Fitter Future for All,’ obesity prevention strategy for Northern Ireland, remains extant, with partners and stakeholders continuing to deliver against the set of 52 outcomes agreed in 2019/2020. Since the close of the consultation on the ‘Healthy Futures’ Obesity Strategic Framework in 2024, my Department has been working through the detail of the responses and looking at the wider research and evidence base. In addition, my Department has been engaging with colleagues across the UK and Ireland to consider emerging developments for those living with overweight and obesity.Work on finalising the consultation response report is complete, and the consultation report will be published shortly. Work to finalise the ‘Healthy Futures’ obesity strategic framework is now well underway, with an aim to publish this in 2025, subject to agreement and the availability of resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) when he will publish a new tobacco strategy; and (ii) what measures this will include to support people to stop smoking, particularly in deprived areas.
answer_text: Preliminary work on a new Tobacco Control Strategy for Northern Ireland had commenced in late 2023 but was paused to allow the Department to focus on supporting the inclusion of Northern Ireland measures in the UK-wide Tobacco and Vapes Bill which is currently progressing through Parliament.  This Bill will be a landmark step in creating a smoke-free UK and a key action in helping to reduce health inequalities. There remain concerning socio-economic inequalities in smoking prevalence across a range of indicators including smoking in pregnancy and exposure to second-hand smoke. Inequalities in smoking prevalence also persist amongst other groups, particularly those with mental ill-health. These issues, amongst others, will also be considered as part of the new Tobacco Control Strategy for Northern Ireland. The Department will continue to focus on supporting the NI measures in the Bill and their implementation, including scoping and bringing forward further regulations under the powers in the Bill.  This will be a substantial programme of work. Officials will then identify the work needed to support this new more comprehensive legislative framework and address the continuing challenges of creating a smokefree Northern Ireland.  This will be the focus of a new tobacco control strategy for NI. Meanwhile, the current Tobacco Control Strategy will continue to provide strategic direction.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the development of the North South Interconnector.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans in place to improve application processes for disability benefits in order to reduce stress and administrative burden on children and young people with cancer and their families, who are already experiencing significant stress and disruption following their diagnosis.
answer_text: Legislation dictates that an application for a disability benefit must be made in the format and manner prescribed. However, my Department has engaged with stakeholders to ensure that families with a child or young person who has had a cancer diagnosis can access the support they need.  As a result, measures have been put in place to assist families through the application process including establishing escalation points for the Royal Victoria Hospital Children's Oncology and Haematology Social Work Team and other support organisations, to fast-track new claims where there is an urgent need. Where required, the Department’s Make the Call service can also visit families to provide benefit advice, assistance with application form completion and signposting to other available support.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any consideration given to streamlining the application process for disability benefits and removing the need for a qualifying period for those whose conditions and their impacts can be evidenced using medical evidence to determine eligibility, such as children and young people with cancer.
answer_text: I refer the member to the answer I provided to AQW 25867/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how his Department ensures that timely financial support is available when it is most needed for children and young people with cancer and their families, in light of Young Lives vs Cancer's The Cost of Waiting research, which states that 57 per cent of these patients in Northern Ireland begin facing extra expenses within the first month of diagnosis.
answer_text: Depending on individual circumstances families facing a cancer diagnosis may be able to access a range of support in the social security system.Where a child or young person has been diagnosed with a terminal condition and has a life expectancy of 12 months or less, access to immediate financial support is provided for in Disability Living Allowance for children or  Personal Independence Payment respectively.My Department’s Make the Call Service can carry out an entitlement check to ensure a person is receiving all the benefits, supports and services available to them. It can be contacted via Freephone number 0800 232 1271 or by emailing makethecall@dfcni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement his Department has had with Young Lives vs Cancer and similar organisations in relation to reforms to the disability benefits system to better support children and young people with cancer.
answer_text: My Department has previously engaged with the B-positive charity and more recently has accepted an invitation to meet with Young Lives vs Cancer to discuss its recently published research report ‘The Cost of Waiting.’

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessment made of the impact of debt on households with a child or young person with cancer, in light of Young Lives vs Cancer's The Cost of Waiting research, which states that 3 in 5 of these affected households in Northern Ireland must borrow money whilst waiting for disability benefits.
answer_text: No such assessment has been undertaken.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to ensure patients have access to routine, non-urgent GP appointments, given that many practices continue to operate on an emergency-only basis despite the lifting of COVID-19 restrictions.
answer_text: As independent contractors and clinical professionals, GPs are responsible for making appropriate decisions about the treatment, care and management of individual patients.  This also means that decisions about the day to day running of the surgery, including the use of technology, telephony and the appointment system, are determined locally by the GP practice within the constraints of their available workforce.My Department gathers data based on weekly survey returns from practices on the number of consultations delivered in Primary Care. This encompasses consultations across the whole practice teams (e.g. GPs, practice employed nurses, TRNs, ANPs, MDT staff, paramedics, practice-based pharmacists) and is not disaggregated by profession.There are approximately 200k patient contacts each week, across the whole clinical team in general practice.Additional measures have been put in place to support primary care to adapt flexibly to increasing demand. My Department has recently announced investment of £61m over the next 4 years to support the wider rollout of the the Primary Care MDT model which will provide access to a wider range of services in Primary Care and will help stabilise GP services. As part of the 2025/26 GMS contract proposals, my Department engaged with the NIGPC on a range of measures to improve the patient experience of accessing GP services. Regrettably, it was not possible to reach agreement with NIGPC on the contract. My Department is now proceeding to implement the 2025/26 GMS contract, which will see an additional £9.5m invested into core GP services. This will include the access improvement measures. The access improvements include a requirement for Practices make some of their appointment slots pre-bookable, so that patients do not always have to call back another time when daily capacity is reached; making sure that Practices make the most of existing digital functionality to make ordering repeat prescriptions easier; requiring Practices to ensure their staff deal with patients appropriately when they ‘walk in’ to make an appointment; and a new requirement for GPs to establish patient participation groups, to give patients a voice in how they are served by the Practice. These measures are based on existing good practice in Northern Ireland, are designed to be implementable by GPs with no additional staff requirement, and are intended to make small but tangible improvement to the patient experience of accessing GP services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the Northern Ireland Audit Office March 2025 report entitled Health and Social Care Imaging Services; (ii) to detail any steps he will take to address the growing gap between demand and capacity for imaging services; and (iii) to detail any financial support he will make available to support the longer-term sustainability of imaging services.
answer_text: I welcome this report, on which my Department has worked closely with the Northern Ireland Audit Office to advise on issues relating to demand and capacity, workforce challenges and the equipment base required for the safe and effective delivery of vital imaging services. The report highlights the work that imaging services have done to optimise productivity, skill mix, quality improvement, extended working and forward planning, and the work that is still needed. It also recognises the significant gaps in funding for annual increases in imaging demand and the subsequent impact this has on service delivery and waiting times for patients. In my statement to the Assembly on 6 May 2025, I detailed how the £215m ringfenced allocation for health in this year’s budget will be invested in waiting list activities. The package of measures to be taken forward includes expanding core capacity in areas such as imaging, where the initial focus will be on increasing utilisation of scanning sessions in core hours, moving to evening and weekend working.In the longer term, the Regional Medical Imaging Board is currently overseeing development of a business case for a multi-professional Northern Ireland Imaging Academy which aims to significantly increase the supply of locally trained consultant radiologists and advanced radiographer practitioners to address the increasing demand for imaging investigations and reduce reliance on costly outsourcing.  While this is a priority area for my Department, progress remains subject to business case approval and the identification of additional recurrent funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether his Department is monitoring the availability of routine GP appointments; and (ii) to detail any actions being taken to support practices in restoring pre-pandemic levels of access for non-urgent care.
answer_text: As independent contractors and clinical professionals, GPs are responsible for making appropriate decisions about the treatment, care and management of individual patients.  This also means that decisions about the day to day running of the surgery, including the use of technology, telephony and the appointment system, are determined locally by the GP practice within the constraints of their available workforce.My Department gathers data based on weekly survey returns from practices on the number of consultations delivered in Primary Care. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any measures his Department is taking to address current and future workforce shortages within imaging services; and (ii) any funding he will make available to update obsolete equipment to ensure the rising demand for services can be met.
answer_text: My Department has already made significant investment to grow the imaging workforce.  Commissioned Radiology medical specialty training places have expanded from a baseline of 37 (2015/16) to 55 (2024/25), an increase of 48.6%. Consideration is also being given to a further increase to 56 places for 2025/26.  Training places commissioned for pre-registration Diagnostic Radiography have also expanded.  A total of 94 places have been commissioned for 2025/26, an increase of 38.2% on the baseline of 68 in 2019/20. In addition, it is intended that a pilot demonstrator public apprenticeship programme in Diagnostic Radiography, with a maximum of 12 participants, will commence in January 2026. However, lack of available funding meant that my Department has been unable to develop the clinical radiology and diagnostic workforce as we would have wished.  A business case is being developed for a multi-professional Northern Ireland Imaging Academy which aims to significantly increase the supply of locally trained consultant radiologists and advanced radiographer practitioners. While this is a priority area for my Department, progress remains subject to business case approval and the identification of additional recurrent funding.Regarding equipment, the Regional Medical Imaging Board monitors the use, configuration and age of all imaging equipment across the entire Health and Social Care system and has developed a schedule for prioritising the replacement of equipment and the procurement of additional equipment in line with population needs and available budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will publish the results of the 2024 Gambling Prevalence Survey for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: A report on the results of the 2024 Gambling Prevalence Survey for Northern Ireland was published on Wednesday 30 April 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how frequently the findings of the Gambling Survey for Great Britain, which collects the region’s official statistics on gambling behaviour in Great Britain, is published.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the Gambling Commission.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the last Gambling Prevalence Survey for Northern Ireland was published.
answer_text: The last Northern Ireland Gambling Prevalence Survey report  commissioned by my Department was published on 3 May 2017.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of the suspension of the Living With Water Programme and the current status of a significant proportion of Combined Sewer Overflows, to detail her (i) long-term; and (ii) immediate plans to address the issue of sewage pollution entering Belfast Lough and across the wider marine environment.
answer_text: With the costs of the NI Water elements of the Plan increasing by over 50% within 2 years of publication it will take longer than the 12 years originally estimated. An Affordability Review of the Belfast Plan confirmed that the wastewater projects in it, will now be taken forward by NI Water at a pace and scale achievable within allocated budgets.  NI Water has subsequently advised that delivery of the upgrades to the major wastewater treatment works around Belfast Lough, have now been paused.  Instead, NI Water will focus on additional maintenance to ensure these assets can continue to operate in the short term, until such times as budget is available to undertake more substantial upgrades.It was always accepted that additional funding would be required to deliver all aspects of the Belfast Plan.  In the New Decade New Approach agreement, Living With Water was identified as a beneficiary of increased funding, but this has not been provided to date by the British government. In this financial year, I have provided NI Water with c£0.5billion funding, however, I will continue to make the case for additional funding for water and wastewater services.  I remain committed to achieving the objectives of the Belfast Plan to develop and implement integrated drainage solutions, including blue / green infrastructure. I will continue to follow my three-pronged approach to secure more investment for wastewater infrastructure, to explore developer contributions and to bring in legislation for sustainable urban drainage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline how he is working with his counterparts in the rest of the UK and Ireland to address the climate crisis.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the potential impact that an expert led, independent Infrastructure Commission will have on capital infrastructure projects.

To ask the Minister of Health, following the March 2025 Northern Ireland Audit Office report Ambulance Handovers in Northern Ireland, to detail any action he will take to improve ambulance handover times.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Audit Office report underlines the seriousness and scale of the pressures facing health and social care services and I note the recognition that the root cause of delayed ambulance handovers is the wider severe health and social care system pressures and hospital discharge delays. Patients are waiting too long in ambulances outside our emergency departments as a direct consequence of poor flow in our hospitals and social care constraints, increasing the risk of harm to patients and also impacting on NIAS’s capacity to respond to emergency calls in the community. While pressures manifest at the Emergency Department, flow through the system and getting those people medically fit for discharge back into the community remain significant issues, meaning that a whole system approach is required. Some progress has been made in delivering changes to services to meet the needs of patients and service users, such as the introduction of Urgent Care Centres and local Phone First services and the introduction of pathways for patients to access a range of services as alternatives to going to an Emergency Department, where this is appropriate.  It is important to reiterate that there is no quick fix and addressing the current situation will require sustained long-term effort, additional recurrent funding and support across the political spectrum. However, I remain determined to find better ways to work as a system to tackle these complex issues. I have asked my officials to look at initiatives used elsewhere and they are engaging with the London Ambulance Service, to understand the model they have implemented with Trusts in the London area which have resulted in improved ambulance handover times. I have also initiated a series of winter planning workshops to identify solutions to the system flow issues that we collectively face. I attended the first of these events on 4 March and was encouraged by the engagement and enthusiasm from HSC leaders across the system. I expect to see better outcomes in advance of next winter, including improvements to hospital handover times.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the Killyglen Link Road area, Larne, will be added to the list of areas to be gritted during periods of snow or ice.
answer_text: My Department routinely treats over 7,000 kms (28%) of the road network, which covers 80% of all traffic movements.  Salting schedules are defined through the application of departmental policy ensuring a consistent application of winter service provision across the north.My Department’s policy confirms that where a parallel route is included in the salted network, then any through route does not require treatment.  However, my officials will carry out traffic surveys on both the Killyglen Link Road and the northern section of the Linn Road, to determine if the gritting schedule requires any amendment for next season.  This may result in the removal of the northern section of the Linn Road to allow for the inclusion of Killglen Link.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 14993/22-27, to define the issues of such importance that their impact is considered to extend to a sub-regional or regional level.
answer_text: As indicated in the answer to AQW 14993/22-27, proposed development and associated circumstances which may be considered to have a regional / sub-regional impact is a matter of judgement on a case-by-case basis. The Department’s Development Management Practice Note 13: ‘Notification and Call-in’ (DMPN 13) provides additional information concerning what areas the Department may consider as part of its assessment of whether or not to call in a planning application. However, this list is not exhaustive and the power to call in applications to the Department for determination is entirely discretionary. DMPN 13 areas for consideration include: the relevant development plan; the opinion of statutory consultees; the national importance of the proposal; its relationship with a Regionally Significant Application; the significance of application to the whole or part of Northern Ireland; and any significant effects outside of Northern Ireland.This document can be found at the following link:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/dmpn-13-notification-and-call-in-v5-may-2016_0.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the current pause of public awareness campaigns, which include organ donation; and (ii) to detail any steps being taken to ensure the continued promotion of organ donation following the change to the opt-out system.
answer_text: You will be aware that I have repeatedly stated that the budget allocated to the health service is wholly inadequate. This resulted in a collective effort across the system and at Department level to make savings, including a moratorium on paid advertising campaigns and consideration of other discretionary spend which has impacted on paid aspects of the organ donation programme. That position remains the same.My Department has a statutory duty to promote organ transplantation under the Health (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.  In line with this duty, my Department oversees a comprehensive rolling programme of outreach and engagement activity to promote knowledge and informed discussion about organ donation, including promoting understanding of organ donation laws, which is aimed at driving registrations on the NHS Organ Donor Register and sustaining and increasing rates of consent to both living and deceased organ donation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 22139/22-27, to detail any assessment made by (i) his Department; and (ii) the Education Authority on the impact that the increase in employers' National Insurance contributions will have on the ability of the approximately 5,000 children with special educational needs and disabilities currently transported by private bus and taxi operators to attend school from April 2025.
answer_text: School transport is one of many vital services provided by private sector employers on behalf of the public sector and I am concerned about the impact the recent Budget may have on the cost to employers of providing those services. The impact on employers of the increase in National Insurance Contributions (NIC) will vary depending on the size of the business, how many it employs and how it manages the new arrangements. I understand that a number of the private bus and taxi operators, who hold contracts with the Education Authority (EA) to provide school transport, are smaller businesses whose NIC liability may not increase or are self-employed and therefore unaffected. The EA does not, however, hold information that would allow it to identify the private bus and taxi operators who will be impacted or to make a detailed assessment of the extent to which this may increase their operating costs. The EA works closely with its transport operators and has advised that to date none have raised concerns about the rise in operating costs due to the increase in NICs, however it will engage with any who are impacted and for whom this presents difficulties.  It is clear that the increase in employers NICs has the potential to increase the already significant cost of the school transport service, however the picture is still emerging. At this stage, I believe the increase in operational costs of those transport operators who are impacted can be managed by the EA through the annual tendering process and I do not anticipate that this will result in children who are transported by private bus and taxi being unable to attend school.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the implementation of Action 29.d of the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031; (ii) to detail the steps taken to ensure that the regional eating disorder service is accessible and effectively resourced across all Health and Social Care Trusts; and (iii) to detail any planned actions to further strengthen support for individuals with eating disorders.
answer_text: The Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 includes a commitment to enhance regional eating disorder services and in line with the Mental Health Strategy Action 29 d, a Regional Clinical Lead and Manager for the Regional Eating Disorder Managed Care Network were appointed in late 2024. They are in the planning phase of establishing the Network and a business case to improve effective resource allocation is in its final stages. A precise timescale for further implementation of Action 29d has not been determined as it will depend upon the availability of funding. The Business Case related to Action 29 d proposes to enhance the current community-based, evidence-based treatment model in line with National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guidance, thereby increasing regional availability and accessibility of intensive community and day treatment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on whether the Independent Living Fund will be reopened.
answer_text: My Department currently provides £7.2m recurrent funding to ILF.  		Following recent meetings between my officials and the Senior Management Team, ILF Scotland, and the Northern Ireland ILF Advisory Group, my officials will shortly be providing me with advice regarding the potential to reopen the Independent Living Funding to new applications in Northern Ireland. 		It remains the case however that due to the very significant and challenging financial position facing my Department, the issues of funding, affordability and sustainability remain key areas for my consideration going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether an assessment has been made of potential savings that could be made within his Department through multi-year budgeting, including the areas the savings would be realised in.
answer_text: Multi-year budgets by themselves don't drive savings but long-term planning will enable us to plan better, set more strategic targets and hence achieve better outcomes. With this in mind I hope that the Executive will soon be in a position to set multi-year budgets for the period 2026-27 onwards.My department is in the initial planning stage of developing a 5-year plan which will align with the Budget Sustainability planning that the Executive has committed to, however we currently are not in a position to identify where any additional savings would come from. The 2025-26 draft budget allocation means that our focus on delivering short-term savings remains and Trusts are currently working to identify the extent to which these can contribute to protecting services in the year ahead.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will commit to delivering a Real Living Wage in social care.
answer_text: As indicated at the Health Committee meeting on 7 January 2025, it is my intention to make the social care sector a Real Living Wage sector. I am planning to commence introduction in the 2025/26 financial year.  This will require additional investment of around £50m, that is, in addition to the £70m invested in the sector in 2024/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether an assessment has been made of potential savings that could be made within his Department through the amalgamation of the five Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: I have already stated on a number of occasions that, given this year’s budget allocation to my Department and the efficiencies that have consequently been required, the priority at this stage is the stabilisation of services.  A focus on an internal reform of structures would detract and distract from the more important issues facing our health service such as tackling waiting lists and addressing health inequalities. In any case, amalgamation of the six Trusts would ultimately only lead to minimal savings as the vast majority of staff cost expenditure would still be required. I have already made clear to all HSC Trusts that I view them as essentially one Trust in practice and that I expect greater cooperation, sharing of limited resources and maximising of possible efficiencies between them.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the funding for the ongoing planned roll-out of multi-disciplinary teams is ring-fenced.
answer_text: The extremely challenging workforce and financial constraints that the Department of Health have been operating under have been a limiting factor in our ability to progress implementation of MDT as widely, and as quickly, as desired. Despite these pressures, as of January 2025, there are over 368 whole time equivalent front-line staff working across 117 GP Practices in 7 GP Federation areas in the Primary Care MDT programme.A proposal to secure funds from NI Transformation Fund, if successful will enable completion of the model in existing MDT areas, and expansion to 5 new GP Federation areas with a population of roughly 670,000 people. This funding is ringfenced investment for transformation .Following the announcement of the 2025-26 Draft Budget allocation, my Department has assessed that Health will be facing a significant funding gap in 2025-26 as a result of both inflationary pressures and increased demand.My Department remains committed to the further implementation of the MDT model across Northern Ireland.  As set out in, Health and Social Care NI – A Three Year Plan to Stabilise, Reform and Deliver, I have made a commitment to publish a plan, by April 2025, for the completion of the Primary Care Multi-Disciplinary Teams (MDT) model across all areas of Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the Stoke Action Plan, (i) whether all eligible patients fulfilling criteria for early supported discharge (ESD) are offered therapy as per guidelines of 45 minutes per discipline available five days per week; and (ii) whether 40 per cent of stroke admissions are discharged to ESD.
answer_text: Targets within the Stroke Action Plan including those listed above, were caveated that additional resource in a challenging financial climate would be required in order to deliver. Whilst ESD is available 5 days per week in all Trusts except Northern Health & Social Care Trust, which requires funding to implement, there is insufficient capacity to offer therapy as per the guidelines shown. Patients are therefore triaged and innovative ways such as group sessions, put in place to maximise activity within funding available.Recent SSNAP date indicated 55% of patients discharged alive following a stroke were discharged to a stroke specific Early Supported Discharge team. This percentage is liable to fluctuation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether the stroke workforce review has been completed; and (ii) when the actions will be implemented.
answer_text: I recognise the crucial role of the workforce in delivering improved outcomes for stroke survivors. Due to resource constraints and the need to take into account revised best practice guidelines, it has not been possible to progress the workforce review within the timescale outlined in the Reshaping Stroke Care Action Plan.However, I can advise that preparatory work to commence the workforce review has been completed. My officials have developed draft Terms of Reference and a project lead has recently been appointed and will join the Department in the coming weeks to take this important work forward. It is anticipated that the review will commence in April. It is expected that the workforce review will take approximately 12 months to complete. Any recommended actions arising from the work of the review will be considered for implementation at that time in the context of available funding and resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the Stroke Action Plan, (i) whether he has met the target of 15 per cent of ischaemic stroke admissions receiving thrombolysis by March 2023; and (ii) whether he is on course to meet the 20 per cent target by March 2025.
answer_text: I am pleased to confirm that Northern Ireland achieved a 15% thrombolysis rate in 2022/23 with 448 patients receiving the acute intervention.However, in line with a changing evidence base using machine learning and clinical pathway simulation, the national target will move from 20% to 18% by 2025/26.  Each hospital will be given its own thrombolysis target which will be monitored through the Sentinel Stroke National Audit Programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the regional model for transient ischaemic attack assessment will be operational, which will deliver a seven days a week service of specialist assessment within 24 hours of symptoms.
answer_text: Targets within the Stroke Action Plan including those listed above, were caveated that additional resource in a challenging financial climate would be required in order to deliver. Expansion of current Transient Ischaemic Attack (TIA) services from 5 to 7 day working with the capacity to respond within 24 hours, will require significant investment in radiology staff and capacity as well as stroke workforce. However, I recognise the importance of TIA management in the prevention of stroke and can advise that regional guidance on TIA management has been developed in 2024 and circulated to HSC Trusts to maximise implementation within existing resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on any action taken by his Department to improve water quality.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the Stroke Action Plan, (i) whether he has met the target of 8 per cent of ischaemic stroke admissions in a stroke-receiving hospital receiving thrombectomy by March 2024; and (ii) whether he is on course to meet the target of 10 per cent by March 2026.
answer_text: The thrombectomy targets set in the 2022 Stroke Action Plan were based on a 24/7 service being in place. Given the budgetary constraints, we have not been in a position to expand thrombectomy to 24/7. This would require significant investment including the recruitment of specialist posts within a limited workforce pool. Therefore the 8% target for 2024 and the 10% for 2026 will not be met. However, I am committed to ensuring as many people as possible from across Northern Ireland continue to benefit from the service within the current hours of operation. The thrombectomy rate of 6% as at March 2024 compares favourably with that for England and Wales.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the impact that the increase in employers’ National Insurance contributions will have on the ability of children with special educational needs and disabilities to attend school through home-to-school transport.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the delivery of school transport and this includes the management of contracts with private transport operators. Transport is provided for around 10,000 pupils with a statement of Special Educational Needs (SEN) who have additional transport requirements and over half of these pupils are transported through the use of the EA’s own bus fleet. Transport for the remainder of these pupils with SEN is provided through contracts the EA holds with private bus and taxi operators.The increase in employers’ National Insurance contributions represents an additional cost to education, including the home to school transport service delivered by the EA. The delivery of transport for eligible pupils, however, is a statutory duty and there is no indication that pupils with SEN will be negatively impacted by this increase. The Chancellor has stated that support will be provided for public sector employers in relation to the upcoming changes to employers' National Insurance Contributions. That said, the quantum has not yet been confirmed by HM Treasury. I can assure you that I will strongly advocate for these additional costs to the education sector to be met in full, as will my Executive colleagues for their departments.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline what steps they are taking to ensure their Department’s communication on social media is accessible to everyone.
To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will conduct research into whether there are any health inequalities faced by people living in rural areas regarding cancer diagnoses.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on progression towards delivering a lung health strategy, including any timescales planned by his Department.
answer_text: I have asked my officials to focus their efforts on the current 4 areas of improving services for adult asthma, interstitial lung disease, children’s specialist respiratory care and diagnostics in primary care. I recognise the need to formalise these work streams into a plan for Northern Ireland, that builds on these priorities and extends into other areas such as Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD), oxygen therapy and others in 2025/26 and beyond. I have asked officials to develop this plan for discussion and presentation by April 2025 and include within that process discussion with the All-Party Group. This plan will be formalised with a reporting structure, lead officials and clinicians, timescales and milestones for transparent monitoring.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the tranche one respiratory working groups (i) adult asthma; (ii) paediatrics; (iii) interstitial lung disease; and (iv) primary care.
answer_text: Tranche one of the respiratory priorities are continuing to progress.  Please find updates on the four working groups below:Adult AsthmaThe Adult Asthma Sub Group members have agreed to use the basis of the ACC Consensus Pathway – Management of Uncontrolled Asthma in Adults produced by the AHSN Network and adjust it for a gold standard service for NI.  This pathway examines various stages of treatment and care for people living with asthma:Indicators of Uncontrolled AsthmaPrimary CareSecondary CareSevere Asthma Centre/Hub for more specialist careThe group noted the main challenge being timely diagnosis and management of asthma.  Members agreed that access to diagnostic testing is fundamental for the intermediate care model to be successful and highlighted an interdependency with the Diagnostic in Primary Care Sub Group work.  The next meeting of Adult Asthma Sub group is scheduled for 20 February 2025.PaediatricsWork is yet to progress on this area.  The working groups (1. Complex Respiratory 2. Sleep and 3. Asthma) are yet to meet as yet as colleagues are exploring if an existing forum, Northern Ireland Paediatric Respiratory and Allergy Network (NIPRAN) would be better placed to lead on this work to avoid duplication and discussions on how this is taken forward are being advanced in Spring 2025. As these pathways reflect NICE guidance with a hub and spoke model with the Belfast Children’s Hospital as the hub, it is felt that building on existing soft networks across paediatrics in NI will be relatively straightforward. However, this group will need to consider additional areas of improvement and therefore investment in 2025/26.Interstitial Lung Disease (ILD) During a series of online workshops over the summer of 2024, members reviewed current levels of service delivery across Northern Ireland and highlighted 5 priority areas for improvement and with that information develop a service pathway for patients. These are:Primary Care EducationStandards of CareProformasData Driven ChangeCommunity and Co-ordinationA short term aim of the Sub Group is to agree an optimal pathway for ILD in NI.  A workshop took place in September 2024 where stakeholders considered and discussed options.  An agreed optimal pathway has now been developed and a further workshop is being organised to take place in April to explore implementation.Primary Care Diagnostics Sub Group The aim of the Sub Group is to develop a new service model for carrying out diagnostics in Primary Care.  Scoping and exploration of a new service model is being carried out and a draft position paper is currently being developed for submission to SPPG to consider this as a priority for investment in 25/26.The 3 options include:Return to the pre-covid model of provisionA GP Practice based hub model A mobile hub and spoke model

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to bring Northern Ireland into line with Great Britain and the Republic of Ireland by setting a target date for a smoke-free society.
answer_text: The Ten-Year Tobacco Control Strategy for Northern Ireland has an overall aim to create a tobacco-free society. A final review of the Strategy, published in September 2023, acknowledged stakeholder support for a smoke-free target date for Northern Ireland. Preliminary work on a new Tobacco Control Strategy for Northern Ireland had commenced late 2023.  Given the introduction of the UK-wide Tobacco and Vapes Bill, the Department’s focus in the immediate term is supporting inclusion of provisions in respect of Northern Ireland in the Bill and its implementation. The Bill offers a unique opportunity to save lives and has the potential to make a significant impact on smoking prevalence rates here. My Department will then identify the remaining challenges, including further consideration of a smoke-free target date, which will be the focus of a successor tobacco control strategy for NI.  It will be important that the setting of such ambitious targets includes considered, resourced and realistic plans as to how to achieve the target. The current strategy continues to provide strategic direction in the meantime.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the respiratory tranche two priorities, including chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, will be taken forward through working groups.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the potential impact of climate change on the local food system.
answer_text: AQO 1492/22-27 Answer: There is no question that the increasing frequency of extreme weather conditions is having and will continue to have wide ranging impacts on the food system in Northern Ireland. Climate-induced extreme weather events, such as flooding and droughts, pose a serious threat to communities by affecting food production, processing, storage and distribution. These heighten concerns over supply chains, food security and livelihoods. We need to develop more circular, secure and sustainable food production systems on a local level. On top of this, resilience will need to be built into supply chains to ensure a fair and even distribution of available food. These threats underline the need for transformations within the agri-food sector and as such my department is investigating potential impacts and mitigations through several strands of complementary research. In the first instance, part of understanding the impact of climate change on our local food systems is understanding what our climate will look like in the future. Then it is necessary to be able to prepare for and mitigate against these predicted changes. DAERA is therefore engaging in a range of research activities related to climate change and food systems. These include research to develop climate projections, investigation into the expansion of sustainable production systems and scenario modelling of novel crops.	Farmers will be key to our future food security. My department’s new Sustainable Agriculture Programme is being co-designed with the Northern Ireland agricultural industry and other key food and environmental stakeholders. Through striving to achieve its four outcomes of improved environmental sustainability, enhanced productivity, stronger resilience and an effective functioning supply chain, this Programme will have a key role in the delivery of my key priorities.Importantly the Schemes being introduced will be essential levers in contributing to Northern Ireland’s statutory obligations under the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022 and achieving a genuinely Just Transition.In addition, the College of Agriculture Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE) is actively addressing the impact of climate change in the agri-food sector. Knowledge transfer and education programmes are targeting the sector’s decarbonisation, focusing on improving the productivity, delivering improvements in environmental sustainability, resilience and supply chain integration. The strong focus on sustainability across CAFRE’s education provision is also equipping new entrants, and those already working within the agri-food industry with the knowledge and skills required to positively embrace the climate change agenda and build resilience.I am focused on ensuring longer term solutions to prevent crises and preserve our food security. As an example, I recently published the Northern Ireland Food Strategy Framework, which seeks to build environmentally sustainable and resilient food systems. The Framework
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the Connect project, based in the Altnagelvin and Ulster Hospital Emergency Departments.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 10 - AQO 1475/22-27		ANSWERCONNECT is an innovative, evidence-led project funded by the Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime (EPPOC) and delivered by the Education Authority. It sees youth workers based in the Emergency Departments of hospitals working with young people aged 15-25, who are often vulnerable to harm and at what the youth workers call a ‘reachable moment’. Currently, the hospitals involved are Altnagelvin, the Ulster Hospital and Craigavon Area Hospital. In 2023/24, alone, the project supported 1,416 vulnerable young people. through practical support and aftercare. Significantly, these follow up sessions often help the young person to develop their capacity for self-support. EPPOC evaluations of the project show that almost half of the young people supported by CONNECT had had contact with the justice system in the three months prior to engaging with the project. After participation in the CONNECT project, young people involved were less likely to attend the Emergency Department, or to engage in unlawful behaviour, or be exposed to violence. They were also less likely to screen for probable depression; and more likely to be employed.The investment in this project is relatively small, but the returns are significant - not just for young people but for the health and justice systems too. This shows that we can work across silos and sectors to help our constituents. This is the way we should be thinking about policy delivery across the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans he has to (i) open diagnostic services twelve hours per day; and (ii) make diagnostic services available seven days a week.
answer_text: Diagnostic services include a wide range of imaging, pathology and other tests which clinicians request in all healthcare settings to help identify illness and determine treatment pathways for their patients.  Many of these services are provided on evenings, weekends and on a 24/7 basis.The 2024 Elective Care Framework and the more recent May 2025 Implementation and Funding Plan, sets out clear proposals for how we will systematically tackle the backlog of patients waiting longer than Ministerial standards.  It will also detail how we will invest in and transform services to allow us to meet the population’s demands in future. The Executive has ringfenced up to £215m in this year’s Health budget for waiting list activities and £12m of this funding has been earmarked for the expansion of diagnostic services. This funding will be used to expand MRI, CT and NOUS capacity across the region in core hours, evenings and weekends, as outlined in the May 2025 Implementation and Funding Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the status of the Integrated Care Hub operating at the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service control centre; and (ii) whether it is fully funded to continue to operate indefinitely.
answer_text: NIAS has fully established its Integrated Clinical Hub (ICH) and it is in operation 24-hours per day, 7-days per week with an ICH Service Manager, 8 Clinical Support Managers (CSM) and 23-24 Clinicians in post.  In addition, Mental Health Practitioners operate within the ICH, in collaboration with the South Eastern Trust, and provide cover on Friday, Saturday and Sunday evenings. All clinicians within the ICH are either HCPC Registered Paramedics or NMC Registered Nurses with at least two years’ post registration relevant clinical experience and complete additional training when starting in post.  This training takes approximately one month and covers remote telephone assessment, the Manchester Triage System, Control room systems and receive 1:1 mentoring and training by experienced clinicians.With senior oversight provided by the ICH service manager and CSMs, ICH clinicians assess calls and direct callers to the most appropriate health care using ‘Hear and Treat’ pathways, upgrade calls for a faster response if required, provide remote clinical advice and support to operational colleagues to use ‘See and Treat’ pathways through shared clinical decision making and provide advice to non-clinical control room colleagues. There has been an increase (48%) in the number of patients treated by the ICH team with 8,982 patients in 2024 compared to 6,087 in 2023.  Recurrent funding is in place for the ICH to continue operation for the foreseeable future, however the Mental Health Practitioner element is only funded until end of the financial year 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what measures will be included in the revised Integrated Education Strategy Action Plan to ensure demand for Integrated Education is effectively monitored.
answer_text: Identifying, assessing, monitoring and aiming to meet demand for Integrated Education is a complex and ongoing process. Demand comprises two distinct elements - Societal Demand and Behavioural Demand.Societal Demand - This is evidence of demand taken from a Northern Ireland wide survey of parents on their desire for Integrated Education and the factors they consider when expressing school preferences and from parental ballots held to consider Transformation proposals. Behavioural Demand - This is evidence of demand indicated by school preference as expressed via the school admission system at entry to pre-school, primary and post-primary school by surveying parental inclination/aspirations of parents and, from parental ballots held to consider Transformation proposals.We will use evidence from the Northern Ireland Life and Times Survey to develop a more rounded understanding of societal level demand for Integrated Education. More importantly, my Department will annually survey parents who have recently made school preference decisions via the EA admissions portal to further explore the demand for Integrated Education and analyse annual school admissions data to build up a thorough picture of demand for Integrated Education across Northern Ireland.Results from parental ballots held to weigh up Transformation proposals will also be considered to further understanding of societal and behavioural demand for Integrated Education as expressed by parents.During 2025, and annually thereafter, my Department will develop and publish a report on demand for Integrated Education informed by the evidence gathered. I expect the EA will use this report to meet its statutory obligation to aim to meet demand for Integrated Education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the winter flu vaccination target for 2024/25; and (ii) the number of people that have received the winter flu vaccination to date.
answer_text: There are no set uptake targets for the various groups included in the annual influenza vaccination programme.  The aim is to ensure the vast majority of those eligible, which is around one million people, are offered the vaccine, in line with JCVI advice, between October and December each year, before the influenza season normally begins. The Public Health Agency, which is responsible for the operational planning and implementation of the annual influenza vaccination programme, has advised that all eligible groups have now been offered a vaccine as part of the 2024/25 programme.  As of 6 January 2025 526,464, influenza vaccinations have been recorded as administered as part of this programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 17476/22-27, whether gambling harm is addressed through teaching on gambling, gaming and financial capability across several areas of the curriculum in (i) all primary schools; and (ii) all post-primary schools.
answer_text: In the Northern Ireland curriculum, the topic of gambling harm is explored through the wider themes of financial capability, risk management and making responsible choices.  While the term "gambling harm" is not always explicitly referenced, the curriculum includes aspects that enable students to comprehend the dangers and outcomes linked to gambling and related behaviours. Financial capability, encompassing awareness of gambling and gaming, is already established as a statutory and essential component of the curriculum.  Opportunities for the teaching and learning of financial capability occur across the curriculum and through young people’s everyday experiences inside and outside school.  At primary level, financial capability is most closely associated with Mathematics and Numeracy, however, it features across all the areas of learning.  For example, under Personal Development and Mutual Understanding, using the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment’s (CCEA) Gambling and Gaming Awareness resource, teachers can help pupils understand what gaming is, the types of games available and the associated risks.  This includes the amount of time spent on gaming, in-game transactions, and the difference between ‘selected’ and ‘random’ (gambling) purchases.At post-primary level, financial capability is further embedded across the curriculum and teachers should provide pupils with opportunities to enhance their knowledge and understanding of a range of finance issues, to develop skills that will enable them to make competent and responsible financial decisions throughout their lives.  Under the Learning for Life and Work (LLW) area of learning, using CCEA’s Gambling and Gaming Awareness resource, teachers can help pupils recognise that gambling and gaming products are designed to make money, and that advertising actively promotes them.  Furthermore, LLW also provides young people with opportunities to explore issues around addiction and its implications for financial wellbeing.  Educating students about the risks associated with gambling is consistent with the objectives of the curriculum.  These aims include promoting critical thinking and informed decision-making, providing students with the necessary skills to navigate risks in their personal and financial affairs, and tackling contemporary societal issues, such as the increasing acceptance of gambling via digital platforms.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of shared care arrangements between patients with attention deficit hyperactivity disorder and their GPs have been terminated in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department does not hold information on shared care arrangements  which may be in place within General Practice.Shared care arrangements are not between patients and GPs, they can, and must be agreed, between GPs and specialist Consultants, be that Health and Social Care (HSC) or private healthcare providers. In all cases both the GP practice and the specialist Consultant recommending the medication must be in a position to meet future monitoring and assessment requirements as set out in the shared care agreement. Decisions on whether or not to enter into a shared care arrangement are based upon the clinical judgment of the GP. When a GP considers that specialist Consultant recommended treatment is clinically appropriate; is content that the treatment falls within their clinical remit; and that if necessary appropriate shared care arrangements have been agreed with the HSC and/or private provider and the patient/carers, then they may issue an HSC prescription. However, in doing so, the GP is accepting clinical responsibility for this decision.
