question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the funding of the Stroke Action Plan.
answer_text: The Stroke Action plan identifies six priorities for improvement. The full implementation of priorities in respect of prevention, thrombolysis, thrombectomy and rehabilitation and long term support would require an additional £10m investment in stroke services. Costs in respect of hyperacute stroke care and the stroke workforce have not yet been identified. The Resource budget available to my Department was insufficient to cover the significant increases in costs we are facing in 2024-25 as a result of pay and price inflation and rising demand. Consequently, there has been insufficient funding to meet all of our pressures. In this context, the focus has been on making improvements in stroke services from within current resources.   Looking ahead, my Department has submitted details of additional funding requirements for stroke reform for future years to the Department of Finance as part of the Budget 2025-26 Process: Information Gathering. This information was submitted to the Department of Finance in October 2024 and will inform the Executive’s consideration of the budget for the three years 2025-28.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) who owns the data held by encompass; (ii) how the data is stored; (iii) whether the data is accessible and searchable by the Health and Social Care Trusts; and (iv) whether the data can be accessed for clinical research purposes.
answer_text: HSC organisations, as data controllers, are responsible for safeguarding the data they contribute to the EPIC supplied Electronic Patient Record (EPR), used by the encompass programme. The system operates under robust legal safeguards to protect NI citizens’ privacy and to ensure that any use of patient data is for individual care or wider public health.  Data is stored within the EPIC EPR, a secure, integrated digital health and care record platform used globally. Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) methods are used to ensure staff can only access data relevant to their roles. RBAC profiles are applied after staff have completed the training appropriate for their job, as identified by HR.  All HSC staff are contractually obligated to ensure that personal data is treated with the highest levels of confidentiality and staff adhere to the HSC Code of Practice on Protecting the Confidentiality of Service User Information, GDPR legislation, and the common law Duty of Confidentiality. A full audit trail of accesses is maintained within the system.Data within the encompass system may be made available for research in accordance with the wider HSCNI Data Strategy (2022-2030) and in conjunction with the HSC Data Institute and the Northern Ireland Trusted Research Environment (NITRE). These bodies will enable the best practice exploitation of information held by the HSCNI to support collaborative research whilst ensuring appropriate safeguards are in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline any business cases that have been submitted to secure funding for the new IT system needed to deliver the Community Pharmacy Strategic Plan 2030.
answer_text: A bid was made for Capital funding for the e-pharmacy programme by SPPG in April 2022 however due to pressures on the departmental capital budget, this has not yet been prioritised. Full delivery of the strategic vision for Community Pharmacy services is subject to securing additional recurrent funding for an enhanced IT infrastructure that is needed for their safe implementation at scale. Specifically, within the e-pharmacy programme, the Go Pharmacy NI project is essential to digitise community pharmacy services and connect embed them within the HSC. The Department published an Investment Plan which outlines the areas for investment in the delivery of the strategy for which funding has been secured, or will be bid for, during the 2024/25 financial year. The plan may be updated during the year if opportunities for further investment arise.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 17600/22-27, to detail (i) the time-bound restrictions for each large town and city; and (ii) the maximum length of time that cars are permitted to park.
answer_text: Time-bound restrictions that are applied across each city and large town in Northern Ireland are set out in a considerable volume of secondary legislation.  This legislation sets out the restrictions in detail, authorises the lengths of road to be used as parking places, the hours of operation and the maximum length of stay.  In addition, each piece of legislation defines any exceptions which apply to these restrictions. Given the large volume of relevant legislation setting out the vast range of parking restrictions applicable within cities and towns across Northern Ireland, a significant commitment of staff resources and time would be required to extract and collate this information at disproportionate cost.  Therefore, regrettably my Department is unable to provide the information requested.Detail relating to the time-bound restrictions within the Controlled Parking Zones in Belfast, Lisburn and Newry can be found in the On-Street Parking Order (Northern Ireland) 2000.In addition, it may be helpful for the Member to know that the Statutory Rules setting out parking restrictions in cities and towns across Northern Ireland can be viewed via the link below with details relating to specific cities or towns found using the search facility:  https://www.legislation.gov.uk/primary+secondary?title=The%20Parking%20and%20Waiting%20Restrictions%20Order%20

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment of whether the departmental allocations in the October Monitoring round will adequately cover the 2024-2025 public sector pay awards.
answer_text: Departments reported Resource overcommitments of £791 million in the October Monitoring round, which included non-contractual public sector pay pressures of some £440 million. Following the Autumn Budget the Executive had £631.5 million Resource DEL available for allocation.  Unfortunately, even with this unprecedented level of additional in-year funding £180 million of departmental overcommitments remained unaddressed following the outcome of the October Monitoring round.Individual Ministers will need to decide how best to utilise the additional allocations to address these overcommitments, which included pay pressures, whilst remaining within their Resource DEL budget allocation.  However, despite these funding challenges we must do everything we can to find the money to cover public sector pay awards.  I remain fully committed to working together with Ministerial colleagues to try and find a way forward in respect of the Public Sector Pay challenges we face.Our public sector workers deserve be paid fairly given their vital role in the provision of our public services and that we will not be able to deliver on our transformation objectives without their support.That is why I continue to ask all Ministers to do what they can to deliver savings and identify these as early as possible so that any funding available can be prioritised for public sector pay awards.I have been clear that I am determined to work collaboratively with colleagues to bring the best possible outcomes for our workers and for our citizens.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will recommence public awareness campaigns encouraging greater understanding of organ donation laws.
answer_text: My Department has a statutory duty to promote organ transplantation under the Health (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.  In line with this duty, my Department oversees a comprehensive rolling programme of outreach and engagement activity to promote knowledge and informed discussion about organ donation, including promoting understanding of organ donation laws, which is aimed at driving registrations on the NHS Organ Donor Register (ODR) and sustaining and increasing rates of consent to both living and deceased organ donation. The programme is delivered by an Organ Donation Promotion Manager, situated in the Public Health Agency (PHA), through a partnership approach with Health and Social Care Trusts, NHS Blood and Transplant, local government, and charity partners and spans the community, voluntary and education sectors. You will be aware that I have repeatedly stated that the budget allocated to the health service is wholly inadequate. This has resulted in a collective effort across the system and at Department level to make savings, including a moratorium on paid advertising campaigns and consideration of other discretionary spend which has impacted on paid aspects of the organ donation programme. However, the PHA has adjusted the programme to have an increased focus on uplifting initiatives using social media and other approaches and seeking new partnership opportunities to ensure continued promotion of organ donation throughout the year. It is expected that paid campaign activity on organ donation will resume once the Executive is in a position to lift the current moratorium.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the development of a Climate Action Plan.
answer_text: Under the requirements of The Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, DAERA is responsible for preparing and publishing a Climate Action Plan (the CAP) for the 2023-2027 carbon budget period on behalf of all NI Departments. Addressing climate change is one of my Department’s key priorities.  My Department’s functions under the Act, primarily relate to transport and infrastructure, although other areas of the CAP fall under or relate to my Department’s remit such as, planning, water, nature-based solutions, energy and public buildings. It is important that the CAP is based on best advice and evidence and is credible, taking into account Just Transition principles.I welcome the recent publication of the consultation on regulations to establish a Just Transition Commission. My officials have been working closely with officials in DAERA on providing the information required for the CAP.As sector lead for transport, I have been focusing on the steps needed to decarbonise the transport sector here.  My Department is responsible for providing a chapter in relation to transport for inclusion within the CAP and has developed its own bespoke Transport Emissions Model (TEM) to inform the evidence base and help decision making on the most appropriate policies and actions.  My three key high level transport policies are: switch - reducing vehicle emissions by switching fuels to low emission alternativesshift – shifting modes of transport away from private car journeys to sustainable travel alternatives reduce - reducing the need for and length of vehicle journeys Whilst I am committed to delivering against the Climate Change Act, including the CAP, I also recognise that the Executive as a whole needs to work collectively towards the delivery of net zero.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the current Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum will consider a statutory requirement for schools to educate pupils on the dangers of gambling as part of the core curriculum.
answer_text: The review of the Northern Ireland curriculum, to be undertaken by Lucy Crehan, will make a series of policy recommendations regarding the purpose, design, specification and implementation of the Northern Ireland Curriculum.  The review will commence shortly, therefore it is too soon to comment on priority areas of inquiry.  It is unlikely that the final report will include detailed proposals on individual course/subject content.  Although not explicitly mentioned within the Northern Ireland Curriculum, gambling harm is addressed through teaching on gambling, gaming, and financial capability across several areas of the curriculum.  At primary level this is considered within Personal Development and Mutual Understanding. At post-primary level this is taught within Learning for Life and Work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how he will ensure that inflammatory bowel disease services are appropriately resourced and staffed.
answer_text: (i) Crohns and (ii) Ulcerative Colitis are forms of Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD) which is managed mostly under the specialty of Gastroenterology but patients may also be referred into General Medicine or General Surgery.Diagnosis of IBD is led by secondary care and supported by primary care pathways and the provision of laboratory tests specifically Calprotectin testing.  In recent years these patient pathways and laboratory tests have enabled clinicians to prioritise outpatient referrals for IBD and better manage patients who are already undergoing treatment for IBD.  Dietitians also play a vital role in managing conditions such as Coeliac and IBS thereby allowing patients with suspected IBD to attend consultant led outpatients for a diagnosis. Once diagnosed, patients are cared for by specialists including clinicians and IBD nurses in each Trust area.  A number of Trusts have appointed pharmacy support for IBD patients who attend in their ED.  There is also a regional IBD interest group which shares good practice and learning across the region. The Department of Health in recent years has increased funding on an annual basis to ensure that all IBD patients, assessed as benefiting from NICE approved biologic technologies, receive treatment regardless of where they live.  Funding has recently been approved for additional staffing to meet the ongoing demand for biological therapies. Patients across NI are able to access these medicines in a timely manner following diagnosis and treatment as prescribed on a regular basis.  IBD nurses provide dedicated patient support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider introducing a single referral to treatment measure to track the patient journey, similar to the system in England and Wales.
answer_text: A move to a single referral to treatment measure is highly desirable because it better reflects the patient experience and what the patient is really interested in, which is the entire journey. From a technical perspective, the new regional encompass system, once fully rolled out to all Trusts, will mean that Northern Ireland will be well placed to measure referral to treatment times.  However further scoping of the capability of the HSC to deliver a referral to treatment waiting time will be required before a decision can be made on its introduction.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action being taken to manage the supply issues of the pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy, Creon, for people with cystic fibrosis who require this treatment.
answer_text: The Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) leads on the maintenance of supply chains to the UK, and works closely with the other devolved administrations, including my Department, the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) and other operators within the supply chain. Through this engagement and UK-wide medicines shortages working groups, my Department is aware of ongoing supply disruptions affecting pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT) treatments, including Creon® preparations. The disruptions to Creon® preparations have been caused by limited availability of the active pharmaceutical ingredient and manufacturing constraints in meeting patient demand. There are national and locally tried and tested mitigations that can be used during periods of supply disruption. Several of these steps have been taken in response to supply disruptions to Creon® and other PERT products.A National Patient Safety Alert was issued by my Department on 24 May 2024, providing healthcare professionals with advice and actions to take to ensure patients can continue to access appropriate treatments. This alert has superseded previous guidance issued to healthcare professionals through Medicines Supply Notifications. Healthcare professionals can also register with the NHS Specialist Pharmacy Service (SPS) which provides up-to-date information on medicine shortages and their anticipated resupply.Additionally, PERT products were added to the UK restricted export and hoarding list on 22 May 2024, and Serious Shortage Protocols (SSPs) have been issued to community pharmacists for Creon® 10,000 and 25,000 capsules. My Department will continue to work closely with DHSC and the other devolved administrations on these UK-wide supply disruptions to help ensure patients and/ or their representatives can continue to have access to appropriate treatments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Interdepartmental Homelessness Action Plans.
answer_text: The Interdepartmental Homelessness Action Plan is designed to complement the Housing Executive’s Homelessness Strategy. It focuses on addressing gaps in non-accommodation services which have the potential to improve the lives, and life chances, of people who are experiencing homelessness and those who are most at risk of homelessness.The Fourth Plan, which contained thirteen actions, was published on 21 September 2023. My Department published the outcome report on 30 September 2024.My officials are actively engaging with colleagues across Departments to develop the Fifth Interdepartmental Homelessness Action Plan and presented a draft to the voluntary and community sector on 25 September.The resulting discussion and feedback will feed into the further development of the next plan which will run until 31 March 2026.I would like to thank my Ministerial colleagues for their commitment to this work. Addressing the issues which contribute to homelessness takes a collective, whole of Government approach.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps being taken to ensure the availability of GLP-1 medication for GPs to prescribe, despite current shortages across the United Kingdom.
answer_text: The Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) leads on the maintenance of medicine supply chains to the UK and works closely with the devolved administrations, including my Department and other operators within the supply chain. There are national and local tools and processes that can be put in place if a supply disruption occurs, including providing advice and guidance to GPs on the actions they can take to help mitigate the impact on patients.Due to an increase in demand for glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1 RAs) for licensed and off-label use, some strengths and preparations of these products have been experiencing limited and intermittent supplies across the UK since mid-2023. As a consequence, several national and local tools and processes have been implemented during this time to ensure patients with type-2 diabetes can continue to have access to appropriate treatments.Through national and local medicines shortage management groups, agreed communications have been issued to healthcare professionals involved in the prescribing and dispensing of GLP-1 RAs, including GPs. These communications have included National Patient Safety Alerts, and as the supply situation of some GLP-1 RA products has improved, a Medicine Supply Notification was issued on 19 March 2024 for semaglutide, dulaglutide and liraglutide, providing updated advice and actions to take to ensure patients can continue to have access to appropriate treatments for their type-2 diabetes diagnosis. The NHS Specialist Pharmacy Service (SPS) also provides advice to healthcare professionals on prescribing available GLP-1 RAs. As well as this, healthcare professionals in Northern Ireland (NI), including GPs and pharmacists, are encouraged to register on the SPS website and use the SPS medicines supply tool, which is updated regularly by DHSC, for the most up-to-date advice on medicines shortages.Additionally, GLP-1 RAs were added to a list which prevents the hoarding and export of medicines from the UK on 17 June 2023. These products remain on the list ensuring any GLP-1 RA stock residing in the UK can be made available to patients.In terms of local mitigations, GLP-1 RAs were added to the NI Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) Authorised Route (NIMAR) list. Addition to the NIMAR list permits the movement of medicines authorised for the Great Britain (GB) market to be supplied to NI. This has ensured patients in NI can continue to have the same access to stock as those in GB. My Department will continue to work with DHSC and the other devolved administrations, through agreed medicines shortage management groups, to ensure any additional steps, deemed appropriate, can be put in place to make GLP-1 RAs available for GPs to prescribe.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether recurrent funding has been secured for the (i) Clinical Lead; (ii) Heart Failure Lead; and (iii) Prevention and Rehabilitation Lead of the Cardiac Network.
answer_text: Recurrent funding has been provided for the Cardiac Network’s Clinical Lead post. Funding for the Heart Failure Lead and the Cardiac Rehabilitation Lead posts has also been provided for the duration of these projects.In relation to the Cardiac Network’s Workplan, service developments have been progressed in the following areas:  The pacing workstream achieved a regional consensus on the referral and management pathway of patients presenting to hospital with bradyarrhythmia. An agreed process has been implemented for escalation and safe inter-hospital transfer of these patients to a regional centre, if required. This was achieved with no additional funding. In line with NICE guidelines, the Rapid Angina Assessment Clinic (RAAC) workstream implemented a regional pathway for the assessment and diagnosis of patients with new on-set chest pain suggestive of stable angina. This was achieved with no additional funding.  The palpitation pathway was expanded to Mid Ulster GP practices. This expansion was cost-neutral due to repurposing of monitors.   Pre-operative guidelines for inpatients awaiting cardiac surgery have been revised and agreed resulting in streamlined access to inpatient cardiac surgery. This was achieved with no additional funding.  Work relating to other areas of the workplan is ongoing with no funding allocated at present. In relation to the regional medical workforce review of Consultants, Specialists and Associate Specialists, to date a regional survey has been completed establishing a cardiology staffing baseline. In light of other Workplan priorities, the aim is to continue the review next year.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people that have had N-terminal pro-B-type natriuretic peptide blood tests to assist in the diagnosis of heart failure in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: There is no data available pertaining specifically to the number of people, whose NT-proBNP blood tests assisted in diagnosing heart failure. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the progress of outstanding repairs required for Larne High School.
answer_text: The Education Authority’s (EA) Maintenance Service is currently investigating ongoing damp issues in classrooms at Larne High School. The building contractor attended the school in September 2024 and EA will liaise with the school regarding the contractor’s recommendations and follow up actions.The EA has initiated an unavoidable minor works regarding the school’s changing facilities, which require a full refurbishment. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the criteria used to determine whether a planning application is defined as regionally significant, for the purpose of calling in planning applications from local councils.
answer_text: Section 29(1) of the Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 allows my Department to direct that an individual application or applications for planning permission be referred (called in) to my Department instead of being dealt with by a council. This power is discretionary in nature and there is no legal limitation on its scope. The current planning policy context for the potential ‘call in’ of an application is provided by the Strategic Planning Policy Statement (SPPS) 2015 which states that the process provides a necessary safeguard where there are applications that raise issues of such importance that their impact is considered to extend to a sub-regional or regional level. It notes that this discretionary power will only be used in exceptional circumstances and that councils are best placed to deal with the vast majority of applications within their own council areas.There are no specific criteria for considering whether or not a planning application should be called in from a council. Each notification, or third party ‘call in’ request, is therefore considered on a case by case basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of cancellations in elective surgery due to the separation of emergency and elective surgery in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the InterTradeIreland budget.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the support his Department is providing to community pharmacies as a result of the fluctuating prices of medication.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Drug Tariff, published monthly, details the reimbursement paid to community pharmacy contractors for drugs and appliances dispensed against HSC prescriptions.Given that prices for reimbursement in the Northern Ireland Drug Tariff are set at the start of the month, if there is significant price instability or price fluctuations in-month for drugs, there is a mechanism to reset the price during the month – this is called the “concessionary price” mechanism.For example, in August 2024, there were 151 concessionary prices awarded in Northern Ireland.From April’24, the previous Health Minister determined that all drugs “on concession” would be Zero Discount i.e. discount clawback will not be applied.In Northern Ireland, unlike other parts of the UK, a special advance is in the pharmacy system for “drugs on concession” in acknowledgement of the volatility of the pharmaceutical supply chain. This has been in place since January 2018 and is currently £3,000 per contractor. Drug reimbursement in Northern Ireland is not on an item per item basis but rather a target amount of margin or retained purchase profit is adopted such that overall, there will be a level of income associated with the reimbursement of medicines i.e. a cost of drugs plus target margin. This is monitored via the Northern Ireland Ongoing Margin survey which is carried out in parallel by colleagues in Community Pharmacy Northern Ireland and the Business Services Organisation.I can advise that the levels of margin delivered over the last three years have been significantly above the (previous) minimum threshold of at least £26.5million: with the latest available figure for 22/23 being £37.6m.In April 2024, my colleague previous Minister Swann agreed to increase this minimum threshold from £26.5m up to £31.5m along with a move towards new processes in 2024/25 to ensure more timely OMS result reporting.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the support being provided to pharmacies in relation to the fluctuation in prices in respect of long-term medications such as Apixaban.
answer_text: The Department of Health (DoH) NI has a statutory duty to remunerate providers of pharmaceutical services in a fair, accurate and prompt manner. It has a statutory obligation under Regulation 9 of the Pharmaceutical Services Regulations (NI) 1997, to compile and publish a statement known as the Northern Ireland Drug Tariff (NIDT). The NIDT, published monthly, sets out the range of dispensing fees available to pharmacists (remuneration), and details the reimbursement figures paid to community pharmacy contractors towards the actual cost of drugs and appliances supplied against health service prescription forms.Given that prices for reimbursement are set at the start of the month, if there is significant price instability or price fluctuations in-month for drugs, there is a mechanism to reset the price during the month – this is called the “concessionary price” mechanism.In August 2024, there were 151 concessionary prices awarded, which included both strengths of apixaban (2.5mg and 5mg), the medication detailed in Mr Donnelly’s question.From April’24, the previous Health Minister (Swann) determined that all drugs on concession would be Zero Discount i.e. discount clawback will not be applied.In Northern Ireland, unlike other parts of the UK, a special advance is in the system for drugs on concession in acknowledgement of the volatility of the pharmaceutical supply chain. This has been in place since January 2018, and has been flexed upwards and downwards over the last 6 years. it is currently £3,000 per contractor. SPPG continue to keep this special advance under review.In Northern Ireland, as in other parts of the UK, reimbursement is not on an item per item basis but rather a target amount of margin or retained purchase profit is adopted such that overall, there will be a level of income associated with the reimbursement of medicines i.e. a cost of drugs plus target margin.The levels of margin delivered over the last three years are detailed below and these have been significantly above the (previous) minimum threshold of at least £26.5million:2020/212021/222022/23Retained Margin (£m)45.438.137.6In April 2024, Minister Swann agreed to increase this minimum threshold from £26.5m up to £31.5m along with a move towards new processes in 2024/25 to ensure more timely OMS result reporting.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) his Department and Health and Social Care Trust plans to deliver day opportunities for people with a learning disability from September 2024; (ii) the number of people with a learning disability that will be supported each week through this; and (iii) how much of this will be delivered by voluntary and community sector providers, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: DoH are currently finalising the Learning Disability Service Model which sets out Learning Disability Services through the lens of regional consistency and supporting need at an early stage in the community. The service model sets out the Department’s vision for transitions, health & wellbeing, meaningful lives & citizenship, families & carers, home, and mental health & behavioural support services.Meaningful Lives & Citizenship in terms of Health and Social Care, aims to continue the expansion of Community-Based Day Opportunities, which will require cross-departmental and multi-agency working. This also represents a shift from traditional hours of services, to enable people with learning disabilities to access more diverse activities on evenings and weekends. There is a notable interface with Day Opportunities and Community Transport, therefore work is underway with DfI to explore opportunities to maximise this service.It is difficult to predict the number of people with a learning disability that will be supported each week, as eligibility for Day Opportunities Services is determined following individual’s assessment of need. A Day Opportunity may be provided in line with an individual’s assessed need. Currently, Day Opportunities data is not consistently captured across all HSC Trusts. Work is commencing to support consistent collation of Day Opportunities data across HSC Trusts. The DOH through the service model plans to carry out a review of the current Regional Day Opportunities Model on a cross-departmental basis, to define the roles across government to enable people with learning disabilities to have a meaningful life. This will include putting in place arrangements for joint resourcing and management of community services.  The Department will also set and monitor KPIs for the HSC Trusts to expand Day Opportunities and the transition away from Buildings-Based Day Services. Health and Social Care Trusts are responsible for commissioning and delivering Day Opportunities for adults with learning disabilities, aligned to assessed needs. HSC Trusts currently commission a range of Community and Voluntary Sector organisations to provide a range of locally-delivered services and opportunities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline initiatives undertaken to support and improve mental health for people living and working in rural areas.
answer_text: Question 14 - Danny DonnellyTo ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline initiatives undertaken to support and improve mental health for people living and working in rural areas.AQO 764/22-27Answer:The Tackling Rural Poverty and Social Isolation (TRPSI) Framework, which has been in operation since 2016, includes initiatives which address loneliness, and improve physical and mental health and well-being. The Farm Families Health Check Programme (FFHCP) in conjunction with the Public Health Agency and the Northern Health and Social Care Trust provides a comprehensive physical and mental health screening and advice service to farmers who visit livestock markets and for rural people who attend a range of community events which the screening vehicle attends annually across Northern Ireland. Where necessary referrals are then made to General Practitioners or appropriate support services. Further to this initiative the Rural Support charity, who are also funded through the TRPSI Programme, has become the “go to” resource for farmers and their families in rural Northern Ireland whenever they have a personal and/or farm business-related crisis. Rural Support provides a range of programmes and services for farmers and farm family members in support of their farm business and personal wellbeing. Their objective is to reduce emotional distress and help alleviate poor mental health triggers which they measure through the use of the established Warwick-Edinburgh Mental Wellbeing scale with onward referrals made to appropriate support services as required.My Department also has a long-standing funding relationship with the Young Farmers’ Clubs of Ulster (YFCU), through which they promote good health and wellbeing for young people in rural areas of Northern Ireland. Finally, the College of Agriculture Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE) provides a Student Support Service, accessible to all learners, which includes a focus on health and wellbeing.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how his Department intends to support allied health professionals to deliver new National Institute for Health and Care Excellence guidelines for stroke rehabilitation, which now recommend at least three hours of therapy, five days a week for stroke survivors; and (ii) what consideration his Department will give to the use and roll-out of new technologies and innovations, such as virtual reality, to enhance intensity and frequency of stroke rehabilitation therapies.
answer_text: I recognise the important role of Allied Health Professionals in providing rehabilitation services for stroke patients. I understand that the recent changes in National Institute of Health and Care Excellence (NICE) will further impact on existing pressures within the stroke rehabilitation service. In the context of these pressures, my Department intends to take forward a Stroke Workforce Review later this year. The Review will identify the future workforce required to deliver a high-quality stroke service and will be informed by best practice guidance including the revised NICE guidelines on stroke rehabilitation.In the interim, my Department is supporting innovative ways of delivering stroke rehabilitation services, including a pilot of multidisciplinary team stroke rehabilitation groups in the South Eastern Trust. In addition, a pilot has also been introduced in the Belfast Trust to support the use of virtual reality in the delivery of Occupational Therapy. An app to support the delivery of Speech and Language Therapy is also currently in development. It is important to note that the introduction of such technology beyond the pilot phase is subject to additional funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence guidelines (i) NG106; (ii) NG136; (iii) NG196; and (iv) NG238 have been implemented across the Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Clinical Guidelines are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here.  Where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. DoH Circular HSC (SQSD) 13/22 sets out process for endorsement, implementation, monitoring and assurance of Clinical Guidelines in Northern Ireland. As there is no dedicated funding source to support the implementation of NICE Clinical Guidelines in Northern Ireland, any resources required are considered against other competing priorities.There are a significant number of recommendations contained within each guideline. It should be noted that not all recommendations are relevant to all Trust areas and some recommendations are predominantly directed at primary care. The extent of implementation on each of the specified guidelines varies across Trusts, however all Trusts are broadly complaint with the guidelines, notwithstanding a number of highlighted challenges which the SPPG will continue to work with Trusts to address.NG106 - Chronic heart failure in adults: diagnosis and managementAll Trusts are reporting that the guidance has not been fully implemented. Issues highlighted include the impact of demographic changes leading to increased demand and the capacity within Trusts to meet the demand.  In particular, heart failure services may benefit from increased numbers of specialist nurses, physiotherapists and pharmacists to ensure timely diagnosis, treatment and review of patients.NG136 - Hypertension in adults: diagnosis and managementThe guideline predominantly applies to primary care however the relevant elements for Trusts are being reported as fully implemented within NHSCT, SEHSCT and WHSCT.  Work is ongoing by SHSCT to achieve full compliance in the coming months. While BHSCT have indicated broad compliance against relevant recommendations, the Trust has highlighted ongoing capacity challenges to meet demand.NG196 - Atrial fibrillation: diagnosis and managementThe guidance is being reported as fully implemented within SEHSCT, SHSCT and WHSCT, while NHSCT has implemented all recommendations relevant to Trust. BHSCT have highlighted workforce and capacity challenges impacting on the implementation of a number of recommendations.NG 238 - Cardiovascular disease: risk assessment and reduction, including lipid modificationAll Trusts are reporting that the guidance has not been fully implemented. However, as this guidance was endorsed in February 2024, the SPPG’s expectation is that Trusts remain in the planning phase, moving towards implementation over the next few months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the £100,000 savings made in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust in 2023-24 from the Travel Contribution Scheme for medical students; and (ii) to detail savings made by the (a) Western Health and Social Care Trust; and (b) the Southern Health and Social Care Trust in 2023-24.
answer_text: The Pilot Travel Contribution Scheme has not yet been evaluated and so no assessment of potential savings has been made.The Pilot Travel Contribution Scheme will be evaluated in Autumn 2024.  Until this validation is complete, it is not possible to outline any savings in individual HSC Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given the savings that some Health and Social Care Trusts have made from the Travel Contribution Scheme pilot, whether he will now make the scheme central to the savings plans of those Health and Social Care Trusts that require it.
answer_text: The Travel Contribution Scheme for medical students in some Health and Social Care Trusts is at pilot stage.  The Scheme will be subject to validation later this year.  Until this validation is complete, it would not be appropriate to comment on any potential savings which may be realised through the Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will work with universities, Health and Social Care Trusts and the MInister for the Economy to secure an increase in the level of support provided to medical students through the Travel Contribution Scheme.
answer_text: The Travel Contribution Scheme for medical students in some Health and Social Care Trusts is at pilot stage.  The Scheme will be subject to validation later this year.  Until this validation is complete, it would not be appropriate to consider any changes to the level of support provided through the Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the impact of encompass on children's chemotherapy services at the Royal Victoria Hospital.
answer_text: The Trust had plans in place, pre encompass go-live, to protect time-critical work, for example, Paediatric patients requiring chemotherapy. To date, one child has had the commencement of their chemotherapy slightly delayed with treatment which was scheduled for Monday delayed until Tuesday.No other children requiring chemotherapy have been impacted during this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what plans his Department has to prioritise the prevention of youth homelessness.
answer_text: The prevention of Youth Homelessness is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.  The Chief Executive has assured me that addressing homelessness and housing need amongst young people and families with children is a distinct priority for the Housing Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on actions taken to address water quality in rivers, lakes and loughs.
answer_text: I am aware that water quality across Northern Ireland is not what it should be, however work to improve the water quality of our waterbodies will require collective action across government, the public and private sectors and also wider society.I continue to engage with Executive colleagues to seek their approval of the draft Environmental Improvement Plan, the first for Northern Ireland, which lays out my commitment to improving water quality.The draft EIP contains the requirement to publish a third cycle River Basin Management Plan for the period 2021-2027 taking an integrated approach to the protection, improvement and sustainable use of the water environment and containing a Programme of Measures setting out the actions required to protect and improve all water bodies – rivers, lakes, marine (coastal and transitional) water bodies and groundwater.Although still in draft, the plan has been prepared and consulted on, however, I will need time to consider it in the context of emerging information, including reports from the Northern Ireland Audit Office, the Office for Environmental Protection and the Lough Neagh Report before bringing it to the Executive for approval.My focus is to complete this programme of work as soon as possible with my vision to have an environment where our water bodies are at “good” status or better, they support biodiversity and contribute to the health and well-being of everyone.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 11434/22-27, (i) how long it is anticipated before a long-term solution is in place; and (ii) to outline the relevant details for such a solution.
answer_text: My department has engaged with Health & Social Care Trusts and established an interim contingency arrangement to ensure continuity of care for the neuromuscular patient group. My officials have anticipated that a long-term solution will be in place from mid-September.The Health and Social Care Trusts, along with SPPG, will continue to work on finalising the development of a long-term solution in respect of adult neuromuscular services across required operational areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 11434/22-27, to detail any contingency plans in place (i) to maintain the supply of Risdiplam; and (ii) to support people who otherwise would have been starting Risdiplam.
answer_text: My department has engaged with Health & Social Care Trusts and established an interim contingency arrangement.  This will ensure continuity of care for the neuromuscular patient group whilst a long -term solution is being sought. This includes maintaining the supply of Risdiplam for those patients currently accessing this treatment, ensuring those patients can continue to access this specialist medication.  The Health and Social Care Trusts, along with SPPG, will continue to work together to ensure care for those who need it.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 11434/22-27, how his Department is communicating with patients with neuromuscular conditions in relation to developments in the provision of Risdiplam in the absence of a long-term solution.
answer_text: The Health and Social Care Trusts, along with SPPG, will continue to work on finalising the development of a long-term solution in respect of adult neuromuscular services, taking account of operational considerations. This will include information on the provision of Risdiplam for patients. Risdiplam is provided through Belfast Trust at present. This is owing to previous arrangement regarding service delivery with an agreed position whereby Risdiplam prescriptions are covered until 31 March 2025 in the context of service handover.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether any functions of his Department are not currently operating as a consequence of the general election campaign.
answer_text: All functions of my Department are continuing to operate ahead of the General Election on 4 July 2024. However, in continuing to carry out those functions, officials will have regard during the formal pre-election period to the “Guidance On Conduct For Civil Servants And Special Advisers In The Northern Ireland Devolved Administration” which was issued by The Executive Office in May 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the work of the task and finish group currently reviewing the audiology service model.
answer_text: SPPG has established Audiology Task and Finish Groups for both Paediatrics and Adults, to work with Trusts across the HSC to get a better understanding of the population’s audiology needs in Northern Ireland. This task group is currently considering referrals into the audiology service and the current workforce availability.The Adult Audiology Task and Finish Group has met on two occasions from its establishment in February 2024 and it is planned that the review should be available by September 2024.A separate Paediatric Audiology Task and Finish Group has also been established, and several meetings have also taken place with this group. The next meeting of the Paediatric Audiology Task and Finish Group is on 19 June 2024. At this meeting the group will consider the outputs from the work on demand and capacity analysis for children.Moving forward is it planned that the two groups will amalgamate for the development of regional pathways and protocols.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether his Department will reform the audiology service model as a priority during this Assembly mandate; and (ii) what consideration he has given to implementing a primary care audiology service as a solution to waiting lists.
answer_text: The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) within the Department of Health, plans and oversees the delivery of Health and Social Care for the population of Northern Ireland. SPPG has established an Audiology Task and Finish Group to work with Trusts across the HSC to get a better understanding of the population’s audiology needs in Northern Ireland. This task group is currently considering referrals into the audiology service and the current workforce availability. Following correspondences from a number of interested parties, I have asked the SPPG to consider a mixed delivery (HSC and community providers) approach to address waiting lists, should this be required beyond that already funded.In the current situation, however, with significant financial challenges, it is important to acknowledge the underlying constraints in developing such a model.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his plans to address the high levels of audiology waiting times in Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: Audiology Services in Northern Ireland have been impacted by Covid, and other general overarching pressures, this has led to waiting times across the region. At the end of last year, the SPPG was asked to establish an Audiology Task and Finish Group to work with Trusts across the HSC to get a better understanding of the population’s audiology needs in Northern Ireland. This task group is currently considering referrals and other demand into the audiology service, capacity available in each Trust and workforce need and availability.This analysis will identify the total demand for audiology services within Northern Ireland, capacity available to manage the demand and the gap within the service in advance of any additionality required to address this.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the Strategic Planning and Performance Group's evaluation of the NI PEARS Plus initiative; and (ii) to detail the anticipated date for the evaluation report to be published.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Primary Eyecare Assessment and Referral Service Plus (NI PEARS Plus) pilot commenced in 2023/24 and ran for thirty-three weeks, with 834 patients accessing the community optometry-based service with acute eye conditions which would otherwise have presented at Belfast HSC Trust Eye Casualty. NI PEARS Plus seeks to build on the successful NI PEARS scheme which currently manages ca. 4,000 patients in community optometry settings, closer to home.As per commissioning specification, the pilot is currently being evaluated for impact, assessing metrics around activity, performance, clinical safety, patient experience and cost-effectiveness. The clinical safety element has been delayed by the work required within BHSCT to prepare for the implementation of encompass but it is expected that the data from the review of the clinical records will be with SPPG to allow the evaluation to be released by end of June 2024.i) Following evaluation, and in consultation with stakeholders, Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) will make a commissioning decision on whether the service should be mainstreamed and introduced regionally, subject to available funding.ii) From the initial data analysis approx. 25 patients per week were diverted from BHSCT Eye Casualty to the NIPEARS Plus service thus releasing capacity in Eye Casualty. Approximately 93% of these patients were managed to completion in primary care with approx. 7% subsequently requiring referral back into Eye Casualty as deemed too complex to be managed in primary care. These figures are subject to validation, and the management and outcomes audited for quality and safety. Any subsequent regional roll-out would be expected to have scaled positive impact on waiting lists and times for acute eye care.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQO 460/22-27, whether he intends to introduce legislation incorporating the United Nations Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities into law before the end of this Assembly mandate.
answer_text: As I outlined in my response to AQO 460/22-27, I am currently considering the next steps to be taken in the development of an Executive Disability Strategy, which I intend to be taken forward at pace. This will include consideration of the need for a review and reform of disability legislation, including consideration of incorporating the UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities into devolved legislation. The making of an Act of the Assembly typically takes a minimum of 3 years from policy development stage to Royal Assent, particularly if there is a high degree of complexity. Review and reform of disability legislation will be wide-ranging and cross-cutting work that will require consideration and agreement across the Executive. Given the time remaining I will not be in a position to bring legislation forward in the current mandate, though preparatory work could begin as a key aspect of the Disability Strategy.Final decisions on the content and the timings of the implementation of the Executive Strategy, including any potential reform of disability legislation in Northern Ireland, will be subject to Executive Agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 10327/22-27, to detail the number of whole time equivalent speech and language therapists are currently employed (i) in hospital acute stroke units; and (ii) in community stroke teams, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.

SHSCT note that 0.65 WTE of the 2.26 WTE are based in the acute hospital Craigavon area, the remainder are in non-acute hospitals. In WHSCT, the community speech & language therapists provide services to hospital stroke units on a sessional basis. This amounts to 1.2 WTE of the 3.0 WTE community staff available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 11434/22-27.
To ask the Minister for the Economy whether there has been any progress in relation to a decommissioner of last resort strategy.
answer_text: Gas Storage: arrangements for ensuring that gas storage projects can be safely decommissioned, in the event of a project owner or operator being unable to do, have not yet been finalised.Offshore Renewable Energy Installations (OREI): OREI policy is an essential part of the overall strategic framework for the development of offshore renewable energy projects in the North.  My Department published a public consultation on OREI Policy Options which closed on 8th March 2024.  This considered potential legislative changes needed and put forward policy options to establish provisions for OREI decommissioning, including decommissioner of last resort as well as safety zones and rights of navigation.  Responses to the consultation are currently being considered and a government response will follow in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether Ritlecitinib will be made available in Northern Ireland for the treatment of severe alopecia areata in people aged 12 and over, given it is available to patients in England, Scotland and Wales.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. In practice, this means that treatments that have been recommended by NICE for routine use in the NHS in England are also available in Northern Ireland.Ritlecitinib (Litfulo®) for the treatment of severe alopecia areata in people 12 years and over, was endorsed by my Department in April 2024 and is available to eligible patients in Northern Ireland in line with the current NICE guidelines (TA958), where infrastructure is in place and the service has capacity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether (i) her Department; and (ii) the Utility Regulator sought external legal advice on the Islandmagee Gas Storage Project in relation to (a) the issue of consent in Article 37 of the Gas (Northern Ireland) Order 1996; and (b) any other issue.
answer_text: The Department sought legal advice from Departmental Solicitors’ Office in 2022 on the Islandmagee Gas Storage Project in relation to (a) the issue of consent under Article 37 of the Gas (Northern Ireland) Order 1996.  The Department also sought external legal advice between 2011 and 2014 on (b) other issues relating to gas storage facilities. The Utility Regulator has sought external legal advice on the Islandmagee Gas Storage Project in relation to (a) and (b).    In providing this response, no waiver of legal privilege is intended by the Department or the Utility Regulator.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) whether the Islandmagee Gas Storage Project requires a Consent to Construct from the Utility Regulator or any other regulatory body; (ii) whether Islandmagee Energy Limited, or any associated person or company, has applied for a Consent to Construct the gas storage caverns; and (ii) whether a Consent to Construct has been issued to Islandmagee Energy Limited or any associated person or company.
answer_text: Under Article 37 of the Gas (Northern Ireland) Order 1996 (‘the Gas Order’), any person who executes works for construction of a gas storage facility requires a consent from the Northern Ireland Authority for Utility Regulation (‘the Utility Regulator’).  The Islandmagee Gas Storage Project requires this consent in so far as it meets the definition of “gas storage facility” under Article 3(1) and Article 37(14) of the Gas Order.  An application by Islandmagee Energy Limited (‘IMEL’) for leave to apply for judicial review of a view expressed by the Utility Regulator - that the gas storage facility IMEL proposes to build at Islandmagee is of a type to which Article 37 applies – has been listed for “rolled-up” hearing before the High Court, Belfast, on 28 June 2024.No.No.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) whether Islandmagee Energy Limited has submitted the financial information required in accordance with Condition 1.2 of its gas licence; (ii) whether there is a penalty for those who do not comply with the conditions of a licence; and (iii) what criteria the Utility Regulator uses to assess the solvency and suitability of companies to hold a licence.
answer_text: The Utility Regulator has not made any determination of non-compliance by Islandmagee Energy Ltd (IMEL) with the requirements of Condition 1.2. of its gas storage licence.There are potential penalties for non-compliance with conditions of a gas licence.  Further details are available on the Utility Regulator’s website at  https://www.uregni.gov.uk/investigations-and-enforcement.  Schedule 2 of IMEL’s gas storage licence (when read with paragraph 4.7 of Schedule 1) sets out circumstances that might allow the Utility Regulator to move to revoke the licence, some of which relate to what might termed ‘solvency’.  The licence is published at: https://www.uregni.gov.uk/publications/islandmagee-energy-limited-formally-called-islandmagee-storage-limited-gas-storage.  Any decision (and the matter is not under active consideration by the Utility Regulator for decision) to revoke the IMEL licence would be (a) informed by the requirements set out in Article 14 of the Energy (NI) Order 2003 and (b) published (with reasons) in accordance with Article 62(1) of the Energy (NI) Order 2003.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 7479/22-27, to detail (i) the contingency plans that are in place; (ii) how they will be activated; and (iii) when they will be activated.
answer_text: As referenced in AQW 7479/22-27, the Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) lead on the maintenance of medicine supply chains to the UK, and there are national and locally tried and tested measures that can be implemented to ensure patients have access to medicines during periods of disruption.Due to manufacturing issues and an increase in global demand for attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) medicines, marketing authorisation holders have notified DHSC of UK wide and global disruptions of their ADHD products. Consequently, several national and local measures have been implemented during this period of disruption.Following notification of supply issues to DHSC, a management plan was agreed by the UK-wide Medicines Shortage Response Group (MRSG) in line with agreed shortage protocols. MSRG is a multi-disciplinary group, including representatives from my Department, that meets fortnightly to discuss shortages that are likely to have a higher risk impact on patients and / or the overall health service. Concerns regarding the supply of ADHD medicines were brought to MSRG in September 2023, and subsequent updates have been provided to the group in relation to the ongoing supply disruptions. MSRG has agreed the UK wide management measures including the communication plans detailed below to healthcare professionals which have been published in Northern Ireland (NI).Information provided at MSRG is shared at a local level through the NI Medicines Shortage Advisory Group (NIMSAG). NIMSAG is chaired by my Departmental officials and includes relevant representatives from across Primary and Secondary Care organisations. This group has led and co-ordinated actions required locally to implement agreed UK-wide mitigations. In line with the communication plans agreed at MSRG and NIMSAG, a National Patient Safety Alert was initially issued by my Department on 28 September 2023, providing healthcare professionals with the required actions to take during the period of disruption to the various strengths and preparations of these medicines-https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-hss-md-48-23.pdf. While the supply of some ADHD medicines has now been resolved, some strengths and preparations are continuing to experience ongoing supply disruptions. As a consequence, several Medicines Supply Notifications have been issued, and these have provided healthcare professionals with updated guidance on the actions to take regarding the various strengths and preparations that continue to be affected by these global disruptions. Both the National Patient Safety Alert and the Medicines Supply Notifications are reflective of messaging agreed by the UK administrations. If the supply situation changes, there may be further discussions at MSRG and NIMSAG and agreed updated communications may be issued by my Department to healthcare professionals.As detailed in AQW 11792/22-27, healthcare professionals are also encouraged to use the Spe

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the action his Department is taking to ensure the continued supply of methylphenidate to people with ADHD.
answer_text: While supply disruptions of some attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) treatments have now been resolved, there continues to be some ongoing disruptions across the UK affecting various preparations and strengths of methylphenidate, with anticipated resupply dates for those products affected to resolve between May and September 2024.As stated in AQW 7479/22-27, the Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC), who leads on the maintenance of medicine supply chains to the UK, works closely with the pharmaceutical industry, Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA), the devolved administrations, including my Department and relevant stakeholders to put in place mitigation measures when any disruptions arise.Guidance, which has been agreed by UK administrations, was issued by my Department to healthcare professionals providing them with actions to take during this period of supply disruption to ensure patients continue to have access to treatments.A list of currently available and unavailable ADHD products has been published to assist healthcare professionals prescribing these medications on the Specialist Pharmacy Service’s (SPS) website-https://www.sps.nhs.uk/articles/prescribing-available-medicines-to-treat-adhd/. Healthcare professionals can also register with SPS to access SPS medicine supply tool for information on shortages and resupply dates of affected products. These resources are updated regularly by DHSC, and my Department has encouraged healthcare professionals to use these resources.If a patient has insufficient supplies to last until the anticipated re-supply date and is unable to source their prescribed strength and/ or preparation of methylphenidate, then the patient should contact their prescriber who can provide them with appropriate advice on their management options.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 10174/22-27, whether any drugs returned to community pharmacies are able to be reused.
answer_text: I would like to advise the member that his question is related to professional standards for pharmacy practice. Medicines returned to pharmacies that have previously been supplied to patients cannot be reused due to patient safety considerations – the public are entitled to expect that medicines supplied from a pharmacy are obtained from a reputable source and are fit for purpose. The quality and efficacy of a medicine is difficult to assess when returned to the pharmacy and so patient safety cannot be assured - there is no guarantee that the medicine has not been inappropriately stored and degraded, with resulting loss of potency.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 10174/22-27, (i) how the estimation of £18 million annual cost for wasted medicines is calculated; (ii) how this compares to the cost of wasted medicines in (a) England; (b) Scotland; and (c) Wales; and (iii) what methods are used for the safe destruction of medicines returned to community pharmacies.
answer_text: The estimate of £18million annual cost for wasted medicines in Northern Ireland was calculated by extrapolation of figures for waste reported for England, in the York Health Economics Report, “Evaluation of the Scale, Causes and Costs of Waste Medicines”. This report concluded that the waste sum represents approximately £1 in every £25 spent on primary care and community pharmaceutical and allied products use. There is currently £450million expenditure on prescribed products in NI.Regarding the cost of wasted medicines in:England, this was reported as £300million in the report referred to above.Scotland and c. Wales, it would be inappropriate to comment on the cost of medicines waste for Scotland or Wales without having firm evidence to base this upon.The Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group manages the contract process to ensure the safe disposal of returned medicines. Clinical waste bins are provided to all community pharmacies and collected on a routine basis by a specialist waste management contractor.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 10174/22-27, what action his Department is taking to reduce the £18 million annual cost for wasted medicines.
answer_text: I would like to assure the member that significant work is being taken forward by my Department to reduce the volume and cost of medicines wastage. The Department is currently working closely with stakeholders and health service staff including GPs and Community Pharmacists to develop a new ‘Valuing Medicines Policy’ to support the cost effective and sustainable use of medicines. A focus of this work is on how medicines wastage can be reduced. Recent emphasis, which can lead to reduced waste, has been on de-prescribing which means for individual patients, some prescriptions for medicines could be stopped, changed or the amount reduced if it has been assessed that the medicine is either no longer needed, is ineffective, inappropriate or unsafe for that patient. These changes will only be made after an assessment of the patient by their healthcare professional, and where it is in their best interests to deprescribe.SPPG has been delivering a successful social media campaign to support de-prescribing in a wide range of therapeutic areas, and includes advice on lifestyle and other symptom specific interventions to allow patients to better manage their conditions. Additionally, patients are being urged to only order the medicines that they need and use and not to ‘stockpile’.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 7480/22-27, what is being done to maintain (i) the adult neuromuscular out-patient care service; and (ii) the continued uninterrupted provision of Risdiplam and the associated required services for eligible patients.
answer_text: My department is aware that the lead adult neuromuscular consultant left his post as at end March 2024. My department has engaged with Health & Social Care Trusts and established an interim contingency arrangement to ensure continuity of care for the neuromuscular patient group, whilst a long -term solution is being sought. The Health and Social Care Trusts, along with SPPG, will continue to work together to ensure care for those who need it. This includes access to Risdiplam for those patients currently accessing this treatment and the development of a long-term solution in respect of adult neuromuscular services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider undertaking a review of the impact of indoor air quality in childcare and school settings on children’s health.
answer_text: Air quality is the lead responsibility of the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) and we continue to work closely with colleagues in relation to health outcomes.The Executive’s overarching strategic framework for Public Health, Making Life Better (MLB), recognises that health and wellbeing is influenced by many factors, including the wider physical environment. MLB includes a commitment to protect and promote good health and wellbeing through working collaboratively to improve air quality to achieve objectives and targets established to protect health.A review of the impact of indoor air quality in childcare and school settings is not under current consideration by my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the impact of indoor air quality on children’s health.
answer_text: Air Quality, both indoor and outdoor, has a direct impact on health and wellbeing and therefore improving air quality can improve health and protect people across Northern Ireland from ill health. Air quality is the lead responsibility of the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) and we continue to work closely with colleagues in relation to this issue.The Executive’s overarching strategic framework for Public Health, Making Life Better (MLB), recognises that health and wellbeing is influenced by many factors, including the wider physical environment. MLB includes a commitment to protect and promote good health and wellbeing through working collaboratively to improve air quality to achieve objectives and targets established to protect health.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they will consult with the Equality Commission to amend equality legislation to ensure it takes account of any amendments to United Nations Conventions which have come into force since the enactment of the Northern Ireland Act 1998.
answer_text: Ensuring compliance with international treaties is a responsibility for all departments.TEO has responsibility for the International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights, the International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights and the Committee on the Elimination of Racial Discrimination.  Engagement with ECNI is standard practice within our Department, particularly when updating the equality legislation we are responsible for.  We were pleased to receive correspondence from the Chief Commissioner recently noting officials’ continued close engagement on the current review of the Race Relations Order.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the value of drugs returned to pharmacies to be destroyed in (i) 2020-21; (ii) 2021-22; and (iii) 2022-23.
answer_text: Medicines that patients return to community pharmacies in Northern Ireland are sent for safe destruction in sealed containers and, as such, it is not possible to definitively assess the value of the medicines inside. However, it has been estimated that medicines wasted in Northern Ireland are valued at £18 million annually.Despite being unable to provide you with information about the value of medicines returned and destroyed I am able, however, provide the following information about the cost for the safe destruction of medicines returned to community pharmacies in Northern Ireland:YearCost of destruction2020-21£426,790.822021-22£529,468.182022-23£625,436.14

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the continued roll-out of Encompass will restrict the data available from Health and Social Care Trusts who take up the programme.
answer_text: Tools provided by encompass will provide Trusts with more detailed real-time data. The encompass programme is working with each Trust as they implement encompass to actively monitor and validate data against baselines supplied and confirmed by each individual trust. It is important that accurate information is available for evidence-based decision making and provision of official statistics. The work currently undertaken in the South Eastern Trust will support other trusts in faster validation processes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will undertake a benchmarking exercise of the pay and working conditions of staff working in the homelessness sector, to compare with the UK and Ireland.
answer_text: The pay and conditions of employees working in the homelessness sector are the responsibility of the employer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given the rise in melanoma cases, whether he has given consideration to banning the use of sunbeds.
answer_text: I acknowledge the concerns about the role sunbeds play in relation to the development of skin cancer and I am aware of proposals put forward in respect of a ban. The Department will give detailed consideration to these proposals when time and capacity permit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the introduction of Charlotte’s Law.
To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the Welsh 3Ps Policy to support people on waiting lists in Northern Ireland.
To ask the Minister of Health whether he will share the results of the consultation on the Re-introduction of Hospital Parking Charges.
answer_text: The Department of Health launched an eight-week consultation on 14 November 2023, seeking views on the re-introduction of hospital parking charges as a fair and appropriate measure to generate additional income for the Health Service.A summary of consultation responses has been uploaded to the Department of Health website.
