question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQO 132/22-27, to detail the criteria used to designate whether the traffic in this area is operating satisfactorily, given the regular traffic congestion on Donaghy’s Lane in particular.
answer_text: The Department’s view that traffic is operating satisfactorily in the Donaghy’s Lane area of Larne is based on inspections, observations, and judgement of the engineering staff.  It should also be noted that developers, as part of their planning approvals are required to carry out a number of road improvements at Donaghy’s Lane/Antiville Road and Ballyboley Road. Drawings showing the full details of these works can be found on the planning portal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to provide resources to roll-out fractional exhaled nitric oxide testing across General Practice.
answer_text: Fractional exhaled Nitric Oxide is included as a diagnostic test for asthma in NICE guideline NG80 'Asthma: diagnosis, monitoring and chronic asthma management'.Funding has been made available to pilot the use of FeNO across Lisburn GP Practices.The aim of this pilot is to scope how FeNO testing can be used to increase diagnostic certainty for patients suspected of having asthma alongside other objective tests. In addition, the pilot will evaluate the usefulness of FeNO testing to help guide clinical decisions around non-adherence or underuse of inhaled corticosteroids when managing the condition.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in order to bring Northern Ireland in line with Great Britain and the Republic of Ireland, whether he intends to set a smoke-free target date.
answer_text: The Ten-Year Tobacco Control Strategy for Northern Ireland has an overall aim to create a tobacco-free society. A final review of the current strategy was published online in September 2023 and work to develop a new strategy is in the early stages of development. The final review of the Strategy acknowledged the support for a smoke-free target date, and this will be further considered as the new strategy is developed. It is important that the setting of such ambitious targets includes considered, resourced and realistic plans as to how to achieve the target. I have recently announced my intention to seek NI Assembly support for inclusion of NI in the UK Government Tobacco and Vapes Bill. The measures set out in that Bill have the potential to make a huge impact on smoking prevalence rates here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps his Department is taking to increase the use of spirometry equipment across primary care.
answer_text: Spirometry is a key step in managing respiratory disease, ensuring the correct diagnosis and therapeutic/referral interventions.  During the COVID19 pandemic provision of spirometry in primary/community settings was reduced.Under current UK Infection Prevention Control (IPC) guidance Spirometry is now not considered to be an aerosol generating procedure (AGP) and therefore level 1 PPE should be worn, namely, a fluid resistant surgical mask (rather than high grade Personal Protective Equipment) would constitute sufficient airway protection for the healthcare worker supervising the spirometry procedure.Consequently, as primary care healthcare workers are not routinely fit tested and provided with high grade PPE (eg FFP3 and Type II Respirators) this has the potential to significantly increase the level of spirometry possible in primary care facilities for patients experiencing respiratory symptoms or those already diagnosed and who require ongoing reviews.Before spirometry is performed by Primary Care practices they should ensure that they review the Infection Prevention and Control Measures for Respiratory illnesses guidance.  General IPC considerations for clinical areas should be considered and include ensuring good air circulation.  Those responsible for providing spirometry are required to have risk assessed the process and ensured that all aspects are safe for both staff undertaking and patients attending the testing.Practices in Northern Ireland also have the opportunity to contract to provide the Northern Ireland Local Enhanced Service (NILES) Chronic Respiratory Conditions.  This NILES amongst its aims is to reduce pressure on secondary care and improve the long-term health and well-being of patients suffering from Asthma and COPD. The use of spirometry to diagnose and manage the ongoing care of this cohort of patients will increase as a result.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail plans for the introduction of regional lung screening programmes.
answer_text: In line with the Cancer Strategy for Northern Ireland (2022-32), I accepted the recommendation from the UK National Screening Committee to introduce a targeted lung cancer screening programme for those at higher risk of disease.We are at the early stages of scoping out what the requirements of this new programme for Northern Ireland will be.  We will be working with colleagues across Northern Ireland and the rest of the UK to establish the resources required and the likely timeline.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the recommendations of the Speech and Language Therapy Workforce Review 2019 – 2029, whether he will commit to ensuring 40 commissioned training places for Speech and Language Therapists will be offered for the September 2024 academic year intake at Ulster University.
answer_text: The commissioning of pre-registration education and training by my Department is reviewed annually, taking into account a range of factors including staffing and vacancy levels, recommendations from workforce reviews, priorities, and affordability.  I remain committed to implementing the recommendations of the Speech and Language Therapy Workforce Review 2019 – 2029 which will require increasing the number of undergraduate training places commissioned each year by 12 to reach an annual total of 40.A budget for Northern Ireland Departments has not yet been set for 2024/25 and accordingly I am unable to make any commitment to expand commissioned places at this time.  I will continue to make the case for this essential investment in our future workforce. However, I expect that the 2024/25 budget will be extremely challenging.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his departmental commitment to ensure the best care for all those living with a respiratory condition.
answer_text: I am pleased to provide assurance regarding the Department of Health commitment to improving respiratory care. Work has been ongoing since last year under the joint management of the Strategic Planning and Performance Group and the Public Health Agency to formulate a refreshed work programme for the future. Partners from across patient groups, general practice and community and hospital services have come together to improve care and treatment pathways. Given the breadth of the work programme that affects so many people living in Northern Ireland, the priorities for 2024 are as follows:	Primary Care respiratory/diagnostics pathways	Interstitial lung disease	Paediatric respiratory disease	Asthma careInitial meetings of the working groups will take place in April 2024 under the chairmanship of leading clinicians and with the support of my officials in the SPPG and PHA. The work programme has been discussed at the All Party Group for Lung Health and it is the intention that the lead commissioner from the SPPG will regularly attend this forum and provide updates to members.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether respiratory health will be prioritised in the Programme for Government.
answer_text: I will continue to work closely with my Executive colleagues and officials across Departments to ensure key areas of health are prioritised within the new Programme for Government. This work is currently in progress and ultimately the Programme for Government will be agreed by the Executive collectively.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the right for rehabilitation for everyone with a lung condition.
answer_text: Rehabilitation for people living with lung conditions in Northern Ireland comes in the form of pulmonary rehabilitation services that are hosted and run by respiratory nursing and therapy teams in each of the local Health and Social Care Trust areas. After assessment for suitability, patients will be referred to a programme comprising lifestyle, dietary and physical activity advice (and often exercise regimes) to support them to better manage their condition. These programmes often last a number of weeks, are based in a community outpatient setting and are additional to any consultant-led and GP-led respiratory service that the patient receives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on discussions with HM Treasury on the status of funding from the UK Shared Prosperity Fund beyond March 2025.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether the administrative preparations are complete for the appointment of a Commissioner for Victims and Survivors.
To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on progress towards introducing an energy bill.
answer_text: Officials in my Department are developing a range of net-zero policies essential to delivery of the Northern Ireland Executive’s Energy Strategy ‘The Path to Net Zero Energy’. That process includes considering whether existing legislation and regulations can facilitate solutions essential to delivery of its objectives. We intend to use existing legislation where possible. In those areas where the legal framework needs updated, I will consider whether primary or secondary legislation is most effective. While the Department has plans for a wide-ranging Energy Bill those areas that require urgent primary legislation, to meet decarbonisation targets, may need to move in advance of it.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what consideration he has given to (i) restricting; and (ii) banning the use of solid fuels.
answer_text: Action to restrict and ultimately ban the use of coal and other solid fuels necessitates a comprehensive approach across government, encompassing the transition to cleaner heating alternatives, the implementation of emission standards, and the promotion of awareness regarding the associated health risks.My officials are actively collaborating with the Department for Agriculture, Environment, and Rural Affairs Air Quality Team, responsible for developing the regional Clean Air Strategy (CAS). There exists a clear correlation between an effective CAS and the phased-out use of coal and certain other solid fuels. By synchronising our approach, we aim to ensure synergy between initiatives to enhance air quality and the strategic decarbonisation of heating. The Executive’s Energy Strategy, "The Path to Net Zero Energy," outlines the policy direction for decarbonising the economy and phasing out fossil fuels, including gas, heating oil, coal, and certain other solid fuels used for heating. An update to progress on the strategy is produced annually and outlines the key strategic priorities and energy policy initiatives which are being developed and delivered with partners across government.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, following comments by the Speaker at the conclusion of Question Time on 12 March 2024, whether he will supply the information requested in AQW 6154/22-27.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I understand that you have already sought and received the information you requested in AQW 6154/22-27 directly from the Chief Executive on the 6 March and 17 April 2024.The Chief Executive advised of the following: “There are 7,236 people currently in receipt of a Discretionary Housing Payment, with 121 of those in receipt of a Discretionary Housing Payment for more than two years.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what discussions his Department has had with other departments in the last 24 months in relation to progressing the draft Clean Air Strategy.
answer_text: My officials have been engaged in collaborative discussions with the Department for Agriculture, Environment, and Rural Affairs (DAERA) Air Quality Team, responsible for developing the regional Clean Air Strategy (CAS) on various occasions during the last 24 months.Discussions centred on taking action to restrict and ultimately ban the use of coal and certain other solid fuels for domestic heating. There exists a clear correlation between an effective CAS and the phased-out use of coal and certain other solid fuels, and the issue necessitates a comprehensive approach across government, encompassing the transition to cleaner heating alternatives, the implementation of emission standards, and the promotion of awareness regarding the associated health risks.  The Executive’s Energy Strategy, "The Path to Net Zero Energy," outlines the policy direction for decarbonising the economy and phasing out fossil fuels, including gas, heating oil, coal, and certain other solid fuels used for heating. An update to progress on the strategy is produced annually and outlines the key strategic priorities and energy policy initiatives which are being developed and delivered with partners across government. Energy Strategy - Path to Net Zero Energy - 2023 Action Plan Report (economy-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what steps he will take to reduce domestic burning in relation to air quality and pollution.
answer_text: The Minister is profoundly aware of the adverse health impacts resulting from the burning of solid fuels, given the release of harmful pollutants into the air. Addressing these public health concerns necessitates a comprehensive approach across government, encompassing the transition to cleaner heating alternatives, the implementation of emission standards, and the promotion of awareness regarding the associated health risks.My officials are actively collaborating with the Department for Agriculture, Environment, and Rural Affairs Air Quality Team, responsible for developing the regional Clean Air Strategy (CAS). There exists a clear correlation between an effective CAS and the phased-out use of coal and certain other solid fuels. By synchronising our approach, we aim to ensure synergy between initiatives to enhance air quality and the strategic decarbonisation of residential heating.The Executive’s Energy Strategy, "The Path to Net Zero Energy," outlines the policy direction for decarbonising the economy and phasing out fossil fuels, including gas, heating oil, coal, and certain other solid fuels used for heating. An update to progress on the strategy is produced annually and outlines the key strategic priorities and energy policy initiatives which are being developed and delivered with partners across government.  Energy Strategy - Path to Net Zero Energy - 2023 Action Plan Report (economy-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW/7659/22-27, to detail the areas of work in the Northern Ireland Cardiac Network's three-year work plan, including funding assigned to the workplan.
answer_text: The key areas within the Cardiac Network’s three-year work plan which are in progress are outlined below:Heart Failure and Cardiac Rehabilitation Strategic Leadership Groups have been established to optimise and standardise pathways across Northern Ireland (NI).NI cardiac catheterisation laboratories capacity and demand modelling, exploring options of alternative and less invasive investigations. Establishment of a Cardiac Pacing Task and Finish (T&F) Group to ensure appropriate management of symptomatic patients presenting to hospital with unstable cardiac rhythms.Planned review of the Rapid Angina Access Clinic Services to ensure patients meet the specified referral criteria and are assessed at a specialist clinic within the agreed standards.  Establishment of a Palpitation Pathway Expansion T&F Group to evaluate and develop primary care palpitation pathways in NI.   Commence a regional medical workforce review of Consultants, Specialists and Associate Specialists, with a view of facilitating cross-Trust working.   Planned review of Clinical Physiologist workforce specific to transthoracic echo. Planned review of NI inpatient pre-operative cardiac surgery guidelines to ensure a streamlined access to inpatient cardiac surgery. Any service developments resulting from these workstreams will be subject to approval and the availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, following meetings of the Respiratory Forum, for an update on progress towards a lung health strategy.
answer_text: Following the disruption caused by the pandemic, in 2023 a short-term working group of representatives from across the HSC, including Trusts, Primary Care and delegates from the three largest charities working in this area met to formulate a preliminary work programme for improvement. The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) and the Public Health Agency continue to lead this work through lead commissioning arrangements. It was agreed to reform the Respiratory Forum, a regional group, to oversee the development and progress of the work programme.  The first meeting of the relaunched Respiratory Forum will take place in June/July 2024.In order to manage the work programme in a time of constrained resource, it has been decided to split the focus of work into two tranches, with the first commencing in April/May 2024. Working groups, co-chaired by clinical leaders across primary and secondary care, will convene to improve care pathways in the following areas in tranche one:•	Respiratory primary care/diagnostic pathways•	Interstitial Lung Disease•	Paediatric respiratory disease•	AsthmaThe second tranche, to be launched in 2025, will focus on the following areas:•	COPD•	Non-invasive ventilation•	Sleep •	Home oxygen/community respiratory services•	Other areas including bronchiectasis, pulmonary rehabilitation and others

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) why the rate of pay for NHS interpreters has not increased since 2006; and (ii) whether his Department will consider increasing the rate of pay in line with the annual Consumer Price Index.
answer_text: Face-to-face spoken language interpreters are independent self-employed contractors who are not employed by the Health and Social Care (HSC) service but are paid for their service through the Business Services Organisation (BSO) at an agreed rate.HSC Interpreters must be registered with BSO and adhere to the HSC Terms of Engagement.  This stipulates an hourly rate of pay as well as a preparation payment.  (i)	The Department is aware that pay has not increased for a number of years and the impact this is having on the valuable service that interpreters provide. You will, however, be aware of the significant financial constraints within the wider Health Service and the challenge that poses to increase interpreters’ pay rates. (ii)	There has been a number of correspondences to my office regarding this issue.  As a result, I have asked the Strategic Planning and Performance Group to review the hourly rate of pay and the preparation fee for interpreters.I will endeavour to do the best I can to come to a suitable resolution.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) when the current rate for student nurse bursaries was set; (ii) why the rate has not increased since; and (iii) what other supports are available for student nurses at university with childcare commitments.
answer_text: the non-means tested element of the nursing and midwifery bursary, paid to eligible students on pre-registration nursing and midwifery programmes commissioned by my Department, was last updated in the 2012/13 academic year. In September 2022, I announced an increase of 15% in some of the means-tested elements of bursary support – dependant’s allowance and parental living allowance - commencing from the 2022/23 academic year. the high overall volumes of applications to these programmes received by the local universities indicates that the current level of bursary support remains effective in attracting sufficient students. This is despite the level of pre-registration nursing and midwifery training commissioned by my Department having been increased to record levels over recent years.Students at Queen’s University Belfast and Ulster University are also able to access university hardship funds should they meet the necessary criteria. Additional funding for these hardship funds has recently been announced by DfE.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the Northern Ireland Cardiac Network's three-year work plan (i) started; and (ii) is due to be completed.
answer_text: The Cardiac Network’s three-year work plan commenced May 2022.  It is due to be completed April 2025. The workplan is kept under regular review to ensure it aligns with emerging issues within cardiac services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the implementation of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether he is aware of the current shortage of ADHD medication and the reasons for this; and (ii) whether his Department has contingency plans in place to mitigate against future shortages.
answer_text: While supply disruptions of some attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) treatments have now resolved, my Department is aware of ongoing supply disruptions across the whole of the UK involving various strengths and preparations of lisdexamfetamine (Elvanse®) and methylphenidate. The supply disruptions have been caused by a combination of manufacturing issues and an increase in global demand for these products.The Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) in England, who leads on the arrangements for maintaining medicines supply chains to the UK, works closely with the pharmaceutical industry, the devolved governments, including my Department, and others operating in the supply chain to help prevent shortages and to ensure that the risks to patients are minimised when they do arise. There are national and locally tried and tested contingency arrangements in place for dealing with any supply disruptions to ensure that patients continue to get the medical supplies they need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action he will take to ensure the continuity of the provision of Risdiplam for people with neuromuscular conditions.
answer_text: NICE TA755, when originally issued in December 2021, initially recommended the use of Risdiplam as an option for treating 5q spinal muscular atrophy (SMA) in people 2 months and older with a clinical diagnosis of SMA types 1, 2 or 3 or with pre-symptomatic SMA and 1 to 4 SMN2 copies. In accordance with DoH Circular HSC (SQSD) 12/22 the Department of Health made provision for patients on this basis.In December 2023, NICE updated its recommendation to include people of all ages with a clinical diagnosis of SMA types 1, 2 or 3 or with pre-symptomatic SMA and 1 to 4 SMN2 copies to take account for the license extension to the marketing authorisation.In accordance with DoH Circular HSC (SQSD) 12/22 which sets out the process for endorsement, implementation, monitoring and assurance in Northern Ireland, the Department endorsed the updated NICE TA as applicable in Northern Ireland on 26 January 2023.My Department have issued a Service Notification to HSC Trusts and other relevant providers and stakeholders on 19 February 2024 setting out the expectations for implementation.Subsequently, hospital clinicians, on behalf of their patients are currently able to make application to the SPPG for the use of Risdiplam for all ages who meet the necessary criteria.The Department is aware that the lead adult neuromuscular consultant is due to leave post at the end of March 2024 and my Department is currently engaging with Trusts to put arrangements in place for the continuation of this treatment for adults in 2024/25 and beyond.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will address the ongoing traffic flow issues at Sandford Avenue leading onto Old Belfast Road, Larne.
answer_text: The proposed road linkage between Sandford Avenue and Old Belfast Road is subject to a Private Streets Determination, so responsibility for its completion rests with the developer.Although this link road has yet to be completed, officials have reviewed traffic movements and flows in the area and are content that it is operating satisfactorily.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health why he is waiting for negotiations with doctors in England to conclude before addressing pay and conditions for doctors in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I have already taken steps to address, in part, medical and dental pay by accepting the recommendations of the relevant pay review body for 2023/24.Industrial action by BMA members is being undertaken across the United Kingdom in support of an above-inflation uplift and a commitment to address pay erosion. Any pay erosion has been a consequence of national fiscal policies over the past decade which have impacted across the public sector. This is a national issue, and it is therefore prudent to await the outcome of wider discussions to make better informed decisions. My Department has committed to further negotiations when we have greater clarity on any final settlement in the ongoing disputes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of how low morale amongst health and social care staff, due to pay and conditions lagging behind those of neighbouring jurisdictions, will impact on the delivery of health services.
answer_text: Staff are the backbone of health and social care services and deserve to be properly rewarded for their work. They have rightly voiced their displeasure over the last financial year at the delay in processing an appropriate pay award due to the lack of political institutions. The return of the Executive and allocation of resources to my Department has enabled me to take immediate action to address this issue. Our HSC staff are to be highly commended for their continued commitment to serving our population despite the challenging circumstances they face.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how he will address issues in relation to pay awards for Health and Social Care workers lagging behind those elsewhere in the UK during the current financial year.
answer_text: This not an issue my Department can address in isolation. Delays in implementation of pay awards are due to budgetary processes locally. The lack of multi-year budgets restricts the ability of my Department to plan for future expenditure. Any potential pay awards must also reflect wider public sector pay policies issued annually by the Department of Finance, and be approved by that Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will personally engage with the British Medical Association on resolving the current dispute over pay and conditions.
answer_text: As I outlined to the chamber on Tuesday in response to your supplementary question, I have already committed to a meeting with the Chair of the BMA(NI). My officials will also continue to engage with the BMA.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of whether the context around pay and conditions for health workers in Northern Ireland is different from that of the rest of the UK, given that residents in parts of Northern Ireland can work for Sláintecare on higher salaries.
answer_text: There is no doubt that we have seen an increase in individuals leaving our service, particularly in the border areas, to take up employment within Sláintecare. I would anticipate that implementation of the long delayed 2023/24 pay award will go some way to improving the situation, but pay is not the only consideration when individuals make their career choices. As part of the proposed AfC pay settlement, my Department will provide a renewed focus on the Workforce Strategy, to improve working conditions across Northern Ireland. Staff well-being, coupled with real transformation of our services, should be our focus at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what progress has been made on the development of a new Nursing Higher Level Apprenticeship Framework.

To ask the Minister of Education whether he plans to introduce legislation, before the beginning of the 2024-2025 academic year, following the review of the criteria for free school meals and uniform grants.

To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department has considered the introduction of grants for energy conservation projects such as (i) insulation; (ii) heat pumps; (iii) solar panels; and (iv) any other method of improving energy efficiency for households.
answer_text: My department is currently preparing a consultation on evidence-based options for a domestic energy efficiency programme in line with Action 4 of the Energy Strategy Action Plan for 2023. The outcome of this consultation will help to inform options regarding the design, rules, and guidance of future support.  In the interim, the department is working with partners such as the Utility Regulator to assess the options for further scaling of up existing energy efficiency programmes. My department is also preparing a public consultation on a low carbon heat support scheme in line with Action 8 of the Energy Strategy Action Plan for 2023.  The outcome of this consultation will help to inform options regarding the design, rules, and guidance of future support for low carbon heating technologies which will include heat pumps.The Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 set out a target of 80% of electricity consumption to be from renewable sources by 2030. Essential to meeting this target will be the establishment of a support scheme for renewable electricity as outlined in the Energy Strategy Action Plan for 2022 and 2023. My officials are currently working on a scheme design that will incentivise investment in larger scale renewable electricity projects. Options for microgeneration support, including domestic renewable generation such as solar panels, will continue to be evaluated as part of our net zero ambitions.Homeowners may wish to seek independent advice on solar panels, heat pumps and other energy efficiency matters from organisations such as the NI Energy Advice Service, the Energy Saving Trust and Citizen’s Advice. These technologies have the potential to provide an attractive payback to homeowners without grant support. Energy Strategy - Path to Net Zero Energy - 2023 Action Plan Report (economy-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) how many people are currently receiving Discretionary Housing Payments; and (ii) how many have been receiving these payments for more than two years.
answer_text: The number of people receiving Discretionary Housing Payments and how many have been receiving payments for more than two years are operational matters for the Housing Executive.You should, therefore, direct your question to the Housing Executive at its dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the impact of the decision to cut funding to the vasectomy service delivered by GPs as part of primary care surgical services; and (ii) whether he plans to reinstate the funding.
answer_text: The Primary Care Elective Service model has historically been sustained through non-recurrent funding.   I recognise the value of the Primary Care Elective Services but due to a significant non-recurrent funding gap and budgetary pressures in 2023/24 it has not been possible to sustain the primary care vasectomy service beyond September 2023.  Despite a reduction in the primary care service, GPs are still able to refer patients to Secondary Care, however I recognise that hospital-based service waiting times are long and addressing lengthy waiting times is a priority.I remain committed to the delivery of GP Elective Services across Northern Ireland, and I am working with my Executive colleagues to secure the funding needed to tackle our length waiting lists

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for a timeline to implement the Strategic Framework to Ending Violence Against Women and Girls.
To ask the Minister of Education (i) what youth work provision is currently funded by the Education Authority in Greenisland; and (ii) what plans are in place to reopen the Greenisland Youth Centre.
answer_text: The Education Authority currently funds Greenisland Statutory Youth Centre, and four registered voluntary youth providers: 1st Greenisland Boys Brigade; 1st Greenisland Scout Group, Greenisland Methodist Girls’ Brigade, and 1st Greenisland Brownies which are in receipt of EA generic non-targeted funding. In addition, an Area Youth Worker for Carrickfergus and Larne has responsibility to support youth programmes in Greenisland where need is identified, this includes current delivery of the Horizons Programme with young people from Greenisland Youth Centre.A Facilitating Life and Resilience Education (FLARE) youth worker is also working with young people in Greenisland Youth Centre to support positive mental and emotional health and wellbeing, and a partnership response has been established to tackle anti-social behaviour and incidents of potential heightened unrest within the Greenisland area involving part-time and full-time youth workers from the centre in partnership with PSNI, Translink and Carrickfergus YMCA.Greenisland Youth Centre is currently fully operational having reopened in April 2021 following closure due to Covid restrictions and renovations. The Centre has remained open since then.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many social homes have been built in the villages of (i) Glenarm; (ii) Carnlough; (iii) Glenariffe; (iv) Cushendall; and (v) Cushendun, in the last 40 years.
answer_text: The Housing Executive have advised that it does not hold information on the numbers of new social homes built in these locations over a 40-year period. The information has been provided for the period 2007/08 to 2021/22 (i.e. since responsibility for the Programme Management of the Social Housing Development Programme [SHDP]) transferred to the Housing Executive in 2007). Between the period 2007/08 to 2021/22:There have been 13 new social housing Completions in Glenarm.There have been 30 new social housing Completions in Carnlough.There have been no new social housing Completions in Glenariffe.There have been no new social housing Completions in Cushendall.There have been no new social housing Completions in Cushendun.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the School Enhancement Programme in East Antrim, particularly the status of the Technical Feasibility Reports for Carrickfergus Grammar School and Greenisland Primary School.
answer_text: There are four School Enhancement Programme school projects within the East Antrim constituency, namely Carrickfergus Grammar School, Greenisland Primary School, Thornfield House and Roddensvale School. As these are all controlled schools, the projects are being delivered by the Education Authority (EA). The current status of each scheme is as follows:Greenisland Primary School – The design team is currently developing the Technical Feasibility Report (TFR) for the project. The Business Case will be progressed on completion of an approved TFR. Carrickfergus Grammar School – The design team is currently developing the TFR for the project. The Business Case will be progressed on completion of an approved TFR.Thornfield House – The TFR has been completed and the business case is in the final stages of drafting.Roddensvale School – The TFR had been completed and a draft business case produced. However, the EA had to advance additional accommodation at the school for September 2022 and September 2023, due to ongoing critical accommodation needs. The TFR for the School Enhancement project is currently being updated to reflect the provision of this additional accommodation. When the updated TFR has been received, the Business Case will be updated by the EA and resubmitted to the Department of Education for approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps he has taken to progress the implementation of safe staffing levels within the health service.
answer_text: I remain committed to the delivery and implementation of the Delivering Care policy for Nursing and Midwifery. I have secured additional funding to expand the clinical workforce and initiated the process to bring forward safe staffing legislation to the Assembly.My Department is committed to sustained investment in growing the local nursing and midwifery workforce to meet ever increasing demands, for example, this year we have commissioned pre-registration nursing and midwifery training places at a record high level, (1,335). In addition, a total of 1,023 nurses have been recruited internationally over the last 4 years.Since January 2020, I have secured additional recurrent funding of £60m to ensure safe and effective staffing for Nursing and Midwifery from the Executive. To date £25 million of this funding has been allocated, delivering approximately 360 whole time equivalent nursing and midwifery posts.In May I announced a £2.1m education and training package which includes expansion of medical specialty training posts, and an additional 41 AHP training posts this year. As Minister, I will continue to do the best I can to deliver for the HSC and the public within the constrained resources I have available.My commitment to introducing a Safe Staffing Bill early in the new Assembly mandate remains. Despite the current uncertainties, work is continuing in partnership with the co-design group and interested stakeholders to ensure that the policy proposals for the Bill are fully developed and agreed.Transformation of health and social care in Northern Ireland is a priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education why children with a statement of special educational needs and special dietary requirements are not included on the eligibility criteria for non-means-tested Free School Meals on the Education Authority website, when they are entitled to them.
answer_text: Registration and application for SEN children with special dietary needs is available on the EA website at https://www.eani.org.uk/financial-help/free-school-meals-uniform-grants/free-school-meals-uniform-grant-eligibilityIn addition on 30 May 2022, the EA issued a letter to all school Principals setting out the entitlement under special diets.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on plans to alleviate traffic flow issues in the Larne West area.
answer_text: My Department currently has no traffic management schemes planned for the Larne West area and has ensured that all housing developments (both constructed and proposed) in the Larne West area have satisfactory access to the public road network.In the case of the most recent planning application for an additional 500 houses, the applicant was required to complete a Transport Assessment which took account of the missing link at Larne Road West, and highlighted the requirement to provide road infrastructure improvements, such as the provision of traffic signals at the junction of Donaghy’s Lane with Antiville Road.  These improvements are conditioned as part of the approval of that application.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Northern Division at dfiroads.northern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.
